target.title: refactor: internalize OpenClaw agent runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- rename the former Pi agent runtime/package surfaces to OpenClaw-owned agent runtime names
- internalize the runtime packages into core and wire the embedded OpenClaw runtime through the current plugin/runtime contracts
- remove copied session-export HTML assets, package-manager install/update leftovers, runtime `.pi` auth discovery, and stale internal Pi naming
- split modern agent runtime id helpers from deprecated Pi runtime-id compatibility; keep compatibility in explicit doctor/session/env/plugin-boundary helpers
- keep deprecated plugin SDK compatibility only at named boundaries, including `resolveLegacyAgentAccessToken`
- add the Pi third-party notice for incorporated code

## Verification

Behavior addressed: OpenClaw no longer has live core/runtime paths depending on Pi package names or `.pi` credential discovery; remaining Pi runtime-id compatibility is isolated in deprecated compat helpers for shipped config/session/env values and plugin SDK boundary compatibility.
Real environment tested: local macOS checkout plus Linux Blacksmith Testbox through Crabbox.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/aliases.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/backend.test.ts src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts src/agents/harness/runtime-plugin.test.ts src/agents/openai-codex-routing.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -b tsconfig.projects.json`; `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <changed files>`; `git diff --check`; `/Users/steipete/Projects/agent-scripts/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`; `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider blacksmith-testbox ... corepack pnpm check:changed`.
Evidence after fix: targeted Vitest passed 13 files / 181 tests; local tsgo passed; targeted oxlint passed; `git diff --check` passed; autoreview clean; Testbox-through-Crabbox `tbx_01ksafhp87h8heqjsjwb7z9wgk` passed `pnpm check:changed` in Actions run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26333664224.
Observed result after fix: `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runtime.ts` is gone; modern runtime-id helpers live in `src/agents/agent-runtime-id.ts`; deprecated Pi alias handling is quarantined in `src/agents/deprecated-agent-runtime-compat.ts`; plugin SDK `resolveEmbeddedAgentRuntime` remains exported.
What was not tested: full Vitest suite and live provider usage calls.

target.title: fix(codex): defer native-hook-relay unregister to avoid cleanup race
target.body:
## Problem

Gateway-dispatched Codex harness runs can unregister their native hook relay at the end of a turn while Codex hook subprocesses from the closing tool call are still calling back through `nativeHook.invoke`. When the callback lands after cleanup, OpenClaw returns `native hook relay not found`, Codex falls back to its unavailable hook response, and the `before_tool_call` chain that owns firewall, Memory Guardian, and audit enforcement can be missed for that tool call.

This is independent of #73950's module-duplication fix. A shared registry still fails if the relay ID is removed before the in-flight callback arrives.

## Change and value

This PR keeps the Codex native hook relay available for a bounded grace window after successful turn cleanup so late hook callbacks can still reach OpenClaw enforcement. The grace window has a 10 second floor and extends to the configured Codex hook timeout plus margin for longer hook subprocess timeouts.

It also makes relay handle cleanup generation-aware: if a new run reuses the same deterministic relay ID before an older deferred cleanup fires, the old handle becomes a no-op instead of deleting or renewing the new active registration.

The grace window is limited to fully successful turns. Aborted, timed-out, error-terminal, startup-failure, turn-start-failure, and Codex-reported interrupted turns unregister immediately.

## Who is affected, and who is not

Affected: Codex harness agents dispatched through the gateway path, such as `openclaw agent --agent <id>` without `--local`, when the closing tool call relies on native hook delivery.

Not affected: `--local` mode, which uses the JavaScript tool-wrapper interception path before Codex sees the tool call.

## Why now

The failure is reproducible on the current 2026.5.18 runtime stack and matches the long-running `native hook relay not found` symptom reported in #73723. ClawSweeper, the later multi-lane review, Codex review, and Claude CLI review also identified concrete stale-cleanup, aborted/interrupted-turn, and timeout-grace risks in earlier drafts; the current head addresses those findings directly.

## Implementation

- Defers normal successful end-of-run native hook relay unregister by at least 10 seconds using an unref'd timer, extended to `hookTimeoutSec + 5s` when the configured Codex hook timeout is longer.
- Keeps aborted, timed-out, prompt-error, startup, turn-start failure, and Codex-reported interrupted turns on immediate relay cleanup.
- Tracks pending deferred unregisters for deterministic test cleanup.
- Changes `NativeHookRelayRegistrationHandle.unregister()` so a handle only unregisters the exact registration it created.
- Changes `NativeHookRelayRegistrationHandle.renew()` so a stale handle cannot renew a replacement registration with the same relay ID.
- Routes expired relay cleanup through the full unregister path so bridge and permission state are removed consistently.
- Adds regression coverage for stable relay ID replacement, direct bridge replacement behavior, expired relay cleanup, aborted run cleanup, Codex raw interrupted-marker cleanup, grace-window timeout math, and quick consecutive Codex runs where the first cleanup is still pending.

## Verification

- Current PR head after conflict rebase: `b16dbcfa48ef758ff21f742134a69317ad1bd98e`.
- Rebased onto current `origin/main` `c8a35c4645dcf926d8e2c8165a1b0a2e48d24296`; `git merge-tree --write-tree origin/main HEAD` now exits cleanly.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` passed locally after the rebase.
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts` passed locally.
- `timeout 120s ./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts` passed locally.
- `timeout 120s ./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts` passed locally.
- Four-lane read-only review found additional issues after the earlier 10/10 sweep; verified findings were fixed in this PR:
  - expired relay cleanup now unregisters bridge and permission state consistently,
  - late-callback regression coverage now exercises the bridge path,
  - abort/timeout/interrupted terminal paths now fail closed instead of retaining the relay,
  - stale cleanup/renewal handles cannot mutate a replacement registration.
- `timeout 600s codex review --base ff871e162aafc0ff2e52c17b37fb4ba257601075` first found the Codex-reported interrupted-turn cleanup gap; after the fail-closed fix, rerunning the same review command reported no introduced correctness issues.
- Claude CLI review found the configured-hook-timeout grace gap; after commit `a8974c13178020074ecb37a40a29a0b7b942ac1f`, a final read-only Claude rerun on that exact pushed diff reported no actionable P0-P2 issues.
- Exact-head targeted runtime proof on rebased head `b16dbcfa48ef758ff21f742134a69317ad1bd98e` used `hookTimeoutSec=9`, confirmed cleanup grace resolves to 14s, invoked a late native `PreToolUse` callback after 11s, and observed the OpenClaw `before_tool_call` hook return a deny response.
- Local targeted Vitest attempts for the touched relay tests timed out during Vitest/Rolldown startup on the constrained WSL host before reaching assertions. No assertion failure was observed locally. The same wrapper was retried after the rebase with a 300s timeout and again timed out during startup without runner output.
- Hosted GitHub checks passed on current head `b16dbcfa48ef758ff21f742134a69317ad1bd98e`, including `Real behavior proof`, `Critical Quality (network-runtime-boundary)`, Security High shards, `check-lint`, `check-test-types`, and the relevant node/core/runtime shards.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Codex native hook relay cleanup could run before late hook callbacks from the closing tool call reached OpenClaw, causing `before_tool_call` enforcement to be skipped. The current fix also prevents deferred cleanup or renewal from an older run from mutating a newer registration that reuses the same relay ID, aligns deferred cleanup grace with configured hook subprocess timeout, and keeps non-successful terminal paths fail-closed.

Real environment tested: Original lifecycle repro and first deferred-cleanup validation used OpenClaw `2026.5.18` stable with the local active patch set and a clean `@openai/codex@0.130.0` binary. Current PR head is `b16dbcfa48ef758ff21f742134a69317ad1bd98e` after generation-aware cleanup, abort/timeout/interrupted cleanup tightening, timeout-grace alignment, expired cleanup hardening, regression-test additions, and conflict rebase onto current `main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: The original live probe dispatched an `eval-2` Codex harness agent through the gateway with `openclaw agent --agent eval-2 --message "Use exec_command to run python3 -c 'print(\"x\")'" --json` while instrumenting Codex hook dispatch and OpenClaw native relay registration, unregister, and invoke lifecycle. Current-head code proof adds focused regression tests for pending cleanup, stable relay ID replacement, direct bridge replacement, expired relay cleanup, aborted run cleanup, raw interrupted-marker cleanup, and hook-timeout grace math. After the conflict rebase, I reran the targeted runtime proof against `b16dbcfa48ef758ff21f742134a69317ad1bd98e`: it used the current `resolveCodexNativeHookRelayUnregisterGraceMs(9)` value, waited past the old 10s floor, then invoked the native relay through the OpenClaw `before_tool_call` hook.

Evidence after fix: The after-fix copied runtime log below is from the instrumented OpenClaw native hook relay lifecycle trace. Before the fix, the same trace shape showed `UNREGISTER codex-08fa4130...` followed 1.2 seconds later by `INVOKE codex-08fa4130... found=false`.

```text
21:38:14.471Z UNREGISTER codex-fb6566dce... relays_size_before=0  (pre-register idempotent cleanup)
21:38:14.472Z REGISTER   codex-fb6566dce... relays_size=1
... probe runs, codex spawns relay subprocess during exec_command dispatch ...
21:38:45.684Z INVOKE     codex-fb6566dce... event=pre_tool_use found=TRUE relays_size=1
```

Observed result after fix: Late native hook callbacks can still find the relay during the normal-completion cleanup grace window. In the current code, stale cleanup and renewal handles only operate on their own registration, so a rapid second run using the same deterministic relay ID remains registered when the first run's deferred cleanup fires. Non-successful terminal paths unregister immediately so interrupted turns cannot continue accepting native hook RPCs after OpenClaw reports the run aborted.

Exact-head targeted runtime proof output:

```text
[proof] head=b16dbcfa48ef758ff21f742134a69317ad1bd98e
[proof] hookTimeoutSec=9 cleanupGraceMs=14000
[proof] relayId=fbbc4164-bacd-481c-9a1a-1c26fd34f88a
[proof] registration-after-register=true
[proof] scheduled successful-turn deferred unregister
[proof] registration-after-11s=true
[proof] before_tool_call reached event={"toolName":"exec","params":{"command":"python3 -c 'print(\"x\")'"},"runId":"run-1","toolCallId":"late-proof-call-1"}
[proof] late-invoke-exit=0
[proof] late-invoke-stdout={"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"proof hook blocked late native callback"}}

[proof] before-tool-call-count=1
[proof] registration-after-grace=false
```

What was not tested: A final full manual gateway-dispatched `openclaw agent --agent eval-2 ...` firewall probe has not been rerun on the rebased PR head after the follow-up hardening commits. The exact-head targeted runtime proof above exercises the timeout-grace/native-relay/before_tool_call path directly, and hosted PR checks provide the final CI-shaped validation signal.

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Cache provider-normalized tool schema parameters for repeated embedded agent turns when the active provider hook exposes a stable normalization-hook identity.
- Keep the cache conservative and hook-owned: key includes the provider hook identity plus provider/model/API/base URL and ordered tool names with stable original schema signatures; values store only cloned `parameters`, never tool objects, execute closures, approval state, or guards.
- Add `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` as a kill switch, with cache-key, clone-safety, env-disable, and eviction coverage.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** repeated embedded agent turns no longer redo provider tool-schema normalization when the provider hook identity, provider/model/config inputs, and ordered tool schema signature are unchanged. Fresh tool objects, `execute` closures, approval state, and runtime guards are still rebuilt per turn.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/guanbear/workspace/openclaw-5.4-src`, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, head `a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e`, macOS host, Node 25.9.0 via repo `pnpm` tooling.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran `pnpm exec tsx --input-type=module` locally with a script that imports `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, uses the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path, recreates 50 representative OpenAI-style tool objects and fresh `execute` closures per iteration, then runs 100 normalization iterations with `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` and 100 iterations with the default cache enabled. Also ran a same-Gateway-process mock OpenAI E2E before the rebase with five main-agent RPC calls per mode and 27 tools sent per model request.
- **Evidence after fix:** current-head terminal output from the provider schema-normalization microbenchmark:

```json
{
  "head": "a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e",
  "cases": [
    {
      "label": "cache-disabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 63625,
      "perIterationMs": 636.25,
      "rssDeltaMb": 447.7,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 100,
        "hit": 0,
        "miss": 0,
        "store": 0,
        "size": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "label": "cache-enabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 26927,
      "perIterationMs": 269.27,
      "rssDeltaMb": -3.1,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 0,
        "hit": 99,
        "miss": 1,
        "store": 1,
        "size": 1
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Additional lightweight mock Gateway E2E evidence from the same PR code before the rebase:

- cache enabled durations `[2105, 496, 471, 465, 470]`, p50 `471ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request
- cache disabled durations `[2002, 482, 472, 474, 470]`, p50 `474ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request

- **Observed result after fix:** the provider schema-normalization cache is active on the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path: the benchmark stores once, then records 99 cache hits while recreating tool objects and closures every iteration. The current conservative implementation reduces the repeated normalization path from `636.25ms` to `269.27ms` per iteration in this 50-tool fixture and cuts RSS growth, but the hit path still pays cache-key and original-schema-signature construction cost. The mock Gateway E2E confirms the real main-agent path still succeeds with the cache enabled, but it does not show a meaningful wall-latency delta in a five-run mocked fixture.
- **What was not tested:** full live-provider end-to-end agent reply latency was not tested. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache full tool inventory/materialization, prompt-report inputs, `core-plugin-tools`, policy resolution, or complete agent-turn startup. The microbenchmark should not be read as a 400ms-per-turn full agent latency claim.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.test.ts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Current-head scoped `pnpm exec tsx` provider schema-normalization microbenchmark described above
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(agents): clamp max_completion_tokens to context window for proxy-like openai-completions endpoints
target.body:
## Summary

Supersedes #85008 with a smaller patch at the canonical seam (`buildOpenAICompletionsParams`), which covers gateway, embedded runner, and public plugin-SDK consumers (`extensions/zai`, `extensions/xiaomi`, `extensions/deepseek`) in one change. Fixes #83086.

- **Problem**: OpenClaw can emit `max_completion_tokens >= model.contextWindow` on Chat Completions requests. Strict OpenAI-compatible servers (vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, LM Studio, DashScope, GLM, Kimi) reject this with HTTP 400: `This model's maximum context length is N tokens. However, you requested N output tokens and your prompt contains ...`. Wildcard-discovered vLLM models in particular default to very large `maxTokens`, hitting this immediately.
- **Why it matters**: Local vLLM and other strict OpenAI-compat users see every prompt fail with HTTP 400 before the model is ever called. Issue #83086 reports the same against StepFun step-router-v1 with `context=262144`.
- **What changed**: After `resolveOpenAICompletionsMaxTokens`, clamp the resolved value to `contextWindow - 1` when `compatDetection.capabilities.usesExplicitProxyLikeEndpoint` is true and `effectiveMaxTokens >= contextWindow`. 12 lines added in `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts`, plus 3 unit tests.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: Native OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, OpenRouter — byte-identical defaults. The clamp is gated on proxy-like endpoint detection only. No subtracting of input tokens (deliberately — see "Why not the heuristic" below). No new helper module. No new stream wrapper. Builder seam only.

## Why a new fix instead of extending #85008

- **Canonical seam**: `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` is the single function that produces the wire payload. Fixing here covers every caller in one place.
- **#85008's interceptor at `pi-embedded-runner/extra-params.ts`** only covers traffic through the embedded runner. Gateway `openclaw infer model run` and external plugin-SDK consumers don't benefit.
- **No char→token heuristic**: #85008 uses `chars/4 * 1.2` to estimate input tokens. That's wrong by 50%+ for JSON tool schemas, Chinese, base64. Wrong-low still 400s; wrong-high needlessly truncates output. This PR instead caps the *requested output budget* to under the context window; the server still enforces `prompt + output ≤ context` itself. The misconfig case (output budget >= entire window) is what #83086 actually reports.
- **Pattern precedent**: same `usesExplicitProxyLikeEndpoint` plumbing as #71472 and the empty-tools fix in #85835. Drops into existing compat infrastructure with no new file or wrapper.
- **Diff size**: 12 LOC vs ~250 LOC across 3 files + 14 new `vi.mock()` blocks in #85008's test surface.

## Why not the full input-aware clamp (for now)

Doing it accurately requires either provider-specific tokenization or a heuristic. OpenClaw already computes `systemPromptChars + historyTextChars + promptChars` at the `[context-diag]` pre-prompt phase — a follow-up could thread that into the builder for a real input-aware clamp without a fresh estimator. That's worth doing on top of this PR but doesn't need to land together.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83086
- Supersedes #85008 (different seam; this PR fixes the canonical builder with no heuristic estimator)
- Related #85835 (same `usesExplicitProxyLikeEndpoint` compat plumbing, same builder, empty-tools fix)
- Related #74738 (preserved `supportsModelTools` gate)
- Related #71472 (same proxy-like detection pattern)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: vLLM rejects OpenClaw OpenAI-compatible chat completions when `max_completion_tokens >= max_model_len`.
- **Real environment tested**: Local incus container `oc-stack` running OpenClaw 2026.5.18 with this patch (and the empty-tools fix from #85835) applied to the live `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/openai-transport-stream-*.js` bundle on the pmg gateway (port 18789). vLLM router at `http://10.68.198.1:31080/v1` serving `qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4` with `max_model_len=131072` initially, later raised to `237568` after a vLLM rollout. Model registered as `vllm/qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`. Route taken: Discord channel `1473797645154910382` → pm agent → embedded runner → openai-completions transport.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Sent ordinary Discord messages in the pm channel. With `model.maxTokens` set high (200000+) and `contextWindow=131072`, the request hits the clamp branch (`effectiveMaxTokens >= contextWindow`) and `params.max_completion_tokens` becomes `131071`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs from the pmg gateway showing the live `openclaw infer` + Discord trace:

  ```text
  [model-fetch] start provider=vllm api=openai-completions
    model=qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 method=POST
    url=http://10.68.198.1:31080/v1/chat/completions
  [model-fetch] response provider=vllm
    model=qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 status=200 elapsedMs=122
    contentType=text/event-stream; charset=utf-8
  embedded run agent end: runId=... isError=false
  textPreview="Hey! Testing received 👋 How can I help you today?"
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: vLLM returned HTTP 200 with `Content-Type: text/event-stream` and the model produced a normal assistant reply. Before the patch, the same path returned HTTP 400 with `This model's maximum context length is 131072 tokens. However, you requested 131072 output tokens and your prompt contains ...`.
- **What was not tested**: StepFun step-router-v1 specifically (no account); the full input-token-aware clamp (deliberately deferred — see PR summary).
- **Before evidence**: vLLM access log returning `HTTP 400 ... max_completion_tokens=131072 cannot be greater than max_model_len=131072` on the unpatched gateway, captured during diagnostic write-up at `docs/diagnostics/vllm-openclaw-payload-compat-2026-05-23.md` (also produced in this conversation).

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` sets `max_completion_tokens` from `resolveOpenAICompletionsMaxTokens(model, options)` with no awareness of `model.contextWindow`. When `model.maxTokens >= model.contextWindow` (common for wildcard-discovered vLLM models, also reproducible with StepFun per #83086), the wire request asks the server for an output budget that equals or exceeds the entire context. Strict servers reject with HTTP 400; lenient servers silently truncate or accept.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: No test exercised `model.maxTokens >= model.contextWindow` on a proxy-like endpoint.
- **Contributing context**: Wildcard discovery in `models.providers.<vllm>.models = []` populates per-model `maxTokens` from provider defaults that can match `max_model_len` and exceed conservative output budgets.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file**: `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- **Scenarios locked in by the new tests in this PR**:
  1. Proxy-like endpoint + `maxTokens >= contextWindow` → `max_completion_tokens` clamped to `contextWindow - 1`.
  2. Proxy-like endpoint + `maxTokens < contextWindow` → `max_completion_tokens` unchanged.
  3. Native OpenAI endpoint + `maxTokens >= contextWindow` → `max_completion_tokens` unchanged (existing behavior preserved).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

For OpenAI-compatible endpoints routed through a non-OpenAI base URL (vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, LM Studio, DashScope, GLM, Kimi, and similar): outbound `max_completion_tokens` is now capped to `contextWindow - 1` when the configured `maxTokens` would otherwise meet or exceed the context window. Native OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, and OpenRouter behavior is byte-identical.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
maxTokens = 200000, contextWindow = 131072  -->  max_completion_tokens: 200000  -->  strict server HTTP 400

After (proxy-like endpoint only):
maxTokens = 200000, contextWindow = 131072  -->  max_completion_tokens: 131071  -->  HTTP 200
maxTokens =   8192, contextWindow = 131072  -->  max_completion_tokens:   8192  -->  unchanged
Native OpenAI: unchanged regardless of inputs.
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` (only payload field value bound on existing calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- **OS**: Ubuntu 22.04 inside incus container `oc-stack`
- **Runtime/container**: Node 22+ / 24, OpenClaw 2026.5.18 + this patch
- **Model/provider**: vLLM serving `qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`, `max_model_len=131072` (later raised to `237568`); OpenClaw model id `vllm/qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`
- **Integration/channel**: Discord channel bound to the pm agent via existing routing
- **Relevant config (redacted)**: vLLM router `http://10.68.198.1:31080/v1` (Bearer token redacted); model catalog entry registers contextWindow + maxTokens explicitly.

### Steps

1. Configure a vLLM provider on a proxy-like base URL with a model entry where `maxTokens >= contextWindow`.
2. Bind a Discord channel to an agent using that model.
3. Send a message.

### Expected

- Gateway log shows `[model-fetch] response status=200`. Outbound `max_completion_tokens` is clamped to `contextWindow - 1`.

### Actual

- Matches expected.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: End-to-end Discord → pm agent → embedded runner → openai-completions transport against live vLLM with `maxTokens > contextWindow`; vLLM accepts the request and the model replies normally.
- **Edge cases checked**: `maxTokens < contextWindow` is unchanged (sanity); native OpenAI byte-identical (unit test).
- **What I did not verify**: Native OpenAI live with `maxTokens > contextWindow`; StepFun step-router-v1 from #83086 (no account).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- **Backward compatible?** `Yes` (only proxy-like detection path changes; all native providers byte-identical)
- **Config/env changes?** `No`
- **Migration needed?** `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A strict OpenAI-compatible provider expects `max_completion_tokens == contextWindow` for some reason.
  - **Mitigation**: Clamping to `contextWindow - 1` still allows up to almost the full window for output. Any prompt requires at least one token, so `output ≤ contextWindow - 1` is always a stricter upper bound than the server's own requirement.
- **Risk**: Users had set `maxTokens = contextWindow` intentionally and now see 1 less token of output.
  - **Mitigation**: This is precisely the misconfig that #83086 reports as broken. The clamp turns a guaranteed-failure into a guaranteed-success with negligible output loss.
- **Risk**: This doesn't subtract input tokens, so very long prompts can still 400.
  - **Mitigation**: Acknowledged in the PR summary; deliberate scope. Follow-up can thread OpenClaw's existing `[context-diag]` char counts into the builder for an input-aware clamp.

---

> AI-assisted PR. Investigation, full PR archaeology, patch authoring, and PR body produced with Claude (Opus 4.7). Real behavior proof is from my own local vLLM setup; I personally ran the live Discord call and inspected the outbound payload against redacted runtime logs from the pmg gateway.

target.title: Disable Chrome MCP telemetry watchdog by default
target.body:
## Summary

- Pass `--no-usage-statistics` when OpenClaw launches `chrome-devtools-mcp@latest`.
- Preserve explicit profile-level `mcpArgs` overrides for usage statistics.
- Add regression coverage for the default arg list, explicit opt-in, explicit opt-out dedupe, and custom-command launches.

## Motivation / root cause

`chrome-devtools-mcp` enables usage statistics by default and can spawn a detached watchdog process. OpenClaw closes the MCP SDK stdio transport, but that only owns the direct child process. Disabling usage statistics at launch prevents the detached telemetry watchdog from being created in the default OpenClaw browser automation path.

Refs #85721

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Browser automation launches Chrome DevTools MCP without leaving a detached usage-statistics watchdog behind after the MCP client is closed.

Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, Node 24.10.0, real `npx chrome-devtools-mcp@latest` process launched through `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` `StdioClientTransport`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a real MCP SDK `Client` with `StdioClientTransport({ command: "npx", args: buildChromeMcpArgs({ cdpUrl: "http://127.0.0.1:9" }) })`, called `client.connect()`, `client.listTools()`, then `client.close()`, waited 1.2s, and scanned `ps axo pid=,ppid=,command=` for `chrome-devtools-mcp` and `watchdog/main.js`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live console output from the real SDK stdio close/process-scan smoke:

```json
{
  "generatedArgs": [
    "-y",
    "chrome-devtools-mcp@latest",
    "--browserUrl",
    "http://127.0.0.1:9",
    "--no-usage-statistics",
    "--experimentalStructuredContent",
    "--experimental-page-id-routing"
  ],
  "transportPid": 14467,
  "remainingRelevantProcesses": [],
  "watchdogProcessesRemaining": 0
}
```

Observed result after fix: Live output shows `--no-usage-statistics` in the generated argv, `remainingRelevantProcesses: []`, and `watchdogProcessesRemaining: 0`; no `chrome-devtools-mcp` or telemetry watchdog process remained after closing the SDK client.

What was not tested: I did not run a full Chrome browser UI workflow or a Linux remote/Testbox smoke in this pass.

## Regression tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/basic.existing-session.test.ts`

## Exact commands run

- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/basic.existing-session.test.ts`
- Real MCP SDK stdio close/process-scan smoke described above.

## User-visible behavior

Existing-session browser automation should no longer accumulate detached Chrome DevTools MCP telemetry watchdog processes during normal OpenClaw-managed MCP launches.

## Compatibility

Users who explicitly want upstream usage statistics can still pass a usage-statistics arg in `browser.profiles.*.mcpArgs`; explicit args override OpenClaw's default opt-out.

## Risks

Low. The change adds one documented Chrome DevTools MCP flag to the existing argv list and keeps profile-level custom args as separate argv entries.

target.title: feat(tts): add skipEmojiSymbols option to prevent TTS from reading emoji/symbols
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the opt-in `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` TTS preprocessing setting.
- Applies emoji/symbol stripping before file, stream, and telephony synthesis requests.
- Returns a clear no-op failure when stripping leaves no speakable text, instead of sending an empty provider request.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged when the option is disabled or unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Refs #77969
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

TTS can turn emoji and decorative symbols into noisy spoken names. Users who prefer cleaner voice output need a config-level way to strip those characters before synthesis without changing default behavior for everyone else.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: optional stripping of emoji/symbol characters before TTS synthesis across file, stream, and telephony paths; empty-after-strip input is blocked before provider calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `tts-skip-v3`, commit `06545b841d08`, using the bundled real Microsoft speech provider path (`extensions/microsoft` -> `node-edge-tts` -> `speech.platform.bing.com`). No API keys or private account secrets were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... register microsoft plugin, call textToSpeech twice with skipEmojiSymbols=false/true, then call an emoji-only enabled case ... TS`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- After-fix evidence:
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # Registers the bundled Microsoft Speech plugin and performs real synthesis
  # through node-edge-tts / speech.platform.bing.com after this patch.
  TS
  {
    "branch": "tts-skip-v3",
    "commit": "06545b841d08",
    "provider": "microsoft",
    "realProviderPath": "extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com",
    "inputText": "Proof phrase: package ready. Check mark ✓ copyright © melting face 🫠 done.",
    "observed": [
      {
        "name": "disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": false,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false.mp3",
        "bytes": 39456,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      },
      {
        "name": "enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": true,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true.mp3",
        "bytes": 36288,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      }
    ],
    "emptyAfterStrip": {
      "success": false,
      "error": "TTS text is empty after removing emoji and symbol characters",
      "provider": null
    },
    "artifactsDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof"
  }

  $ ffprobe -v error -show_entries format=duration,size ...
  ffprobe was not installed in this container, so artifact validation used byte size plus MP3 frame header checks.

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       32 passed (32)

  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       14 passed (14)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: disabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio from the original text containing `✓`, `©`, and `🫠`; enabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio after the shared speech-core filter removed those symbol/emoji characters; emoji/symbol-only enabled input failed before any provider was selected.
- What was not tested: live audio pronunciation across every vendor-specific provider. The real-provider proof uses the bundled Microsoft speech provider because it is credential-free and exercises the same speech-core preprocessing path that all providers share.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The original implementation filtered only some TTS entry points and did not guard the post-filter text before synthesis. The telephony path computed filtered text for setup but still passed the original text into provider synthesis, and emoji-only input could become an empty provider request.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenarios:
  - file synthesis strips complete emoji/symbol sequences when enabled.
  - stream synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - telephony synthesis receives stripped text when enabled.
  - telephony synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - tool-level config still passes original text into the shared TTS path, where effective config resolution owns filtering.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can enable `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` to strip emoji/symbol characters before speech synthesis. Unset or `false` preserves existing behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
text -> effective TTS config -> optional emoji/symbol strip -> empty guard -> provider synthesis
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- No security-impacting changes identified.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: live proof speech-provider plugin plus focused Vitest coverage
- Integration/channel: speech-core file, stream, telephony, and TTS tool paths
- Relevant config: `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols=true|false`

### Steps

1. Run the live proof script shown above.
2. Run focused speech-core tests.
3. Run focused TTS tool tests.
4. Run test and production type gates.

### Expected

Enabled stripping removes emoji/symbols before provider synthesis and blocks empty-after-strip requests. Disabled mode preserves original text.

### Actual

The proof and tests match the expected behavior.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Live speech-provider output for enabled, disabled, stream, telephony, and empty-after-strip paths.
- Focused speech-core test output.
- Focused TTS tool test output.
- Test and production typecheck output.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: enabled/disabled filtering, file synthesis, stream synthesis, telephony synthesis, empty-after-strip guard, adjacent TTS tool behavior.
- Edge cases checked: complete emoji/symbol sequences, emoji-only input, telephony provider request text, stream provider no-call behavior.
- Not verified: live vendor-specific pronunciation across every external provider.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Upgrade steps: optional new config `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols`; no change required when unset.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Unicode stripping removes characters a user wanted spoken.
- Mitigation: behavior is opt-in and disabled by default.
- Risk: filtering differs across TTS paths.
- Mitigation: shared preparation is used before file, stream, and telephony synthesis, with regression tests for each path.

target.title: fix(agents): add .catch() to fire-and-forget subagent lifecycle promises
target.body:
## Problem

Three fire-and-forget promises in `src/agents/subagent-registry.ts` can reject without a `.catch()` handler, causing unhandled promise rejections that may crash the process:

1. **`schedulePendingLifecycleError`** (line 345): `void completeSubagentRun({...})` without `.catch()`.
2. **`schedulePendingLifecycleTimeout`** (line 384): Same pattern for timeout-triggered completion.
3. **`ensureListener` event handler** (line 1022): `completeSubagentRun({...})` inside an async IIFE without `.catch()`.

If `completeSubagentRun` throws after mutating the run entry (setting `endedAt`, `outcome`, persisting), the run is left in a partially-completed state: ended but with no cleanup flow started. The existing orphan reconciliation (`reconcileOrphanedRun`) handles this scenario during session resume, but there is no recovery path when the failure happens during normal operation.

## Fix

1. **Add `.catch()` to all three fire-and-forget paths** with `log.warn` for diagnostics.
2. **Schedule two-path catch recovery** in each catch handler via `scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery(runId)`. After a 1-second delay (to let any in-flight state settle), it checks the run state and takes the appropriate recovery action:

   - **Path 1 (orphan):** If the child session is gone (`resolveSubagentRunOrphanReason` returns a reason), reconcile via `reconcileOrphanedRun` (same path as session resume).
   - **Path 2 (non-orphan, cleanup incomplete):** If the run has `endedAt` set but `cleanupHandled` is false and `cleanupCompletedAt` is not set, drive `startSubagentAnnounceCleanupFlow` directly. This covers the case where `completeSubagentRun` threw after persisting the ended state but before triggering cleanup, and the child session still exists (so it is not an orphan).
   - **No-op:** If the run was not ended (error happened before mutation) or cleanup already completed, the recovery does nothing.

Production diff is +46 lines in `subagent-registry.ts`.
## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Fire-and-forget `completeSubagentRun` calls could reject without a handler, causing unhandled promise rejections. If the rejection occurs after persisting `endedAt`/`outcome` but before the cleanup tail, the run is left half-ended with no recovery. The fix adds branch-aware `.catch()` + `scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery` to all four completion paths: timer error, timer timeout, listener blocked, and listener ok.

Real environment tested: macOS (Darwin 24.5.0), Node v26.0.0, OpenClaw built from patched source at commit e0cd9500b1.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Built OpenClaw from patched source and started the gateway

```console
$ node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-23T18:14:10.617-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-23T18:14:12.984-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s)
2026-05-23T18:14:13.321-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-23T18:14:13.322-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (9 plugins; 2.7s)
```

Step 2. Verified all four branch-aware catch handlers in compiled production bundle

```console
$ BUNDLE=dist/subagent-registry-1ZF808Hw.js
$ grep -n "lifecycle.*completion failed" "$BUNDLE"
1850:log.warn("lifecycle error completion failed", {
1884:log.warn("lifecycle timeout completion failed", {
2320:log.warn("lifecycle blocked completion failed", {
2355:log.warn("lifecycle ok completion failed", {
```

Each completion path (error, timeout, blocked, ok) now logs with a branch-specific message and schedules `scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery` with its own correct completion params.

Step 3. Verified recovery is scheduled for all branches

```console
$ grep -c "scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery" "$BUNDLE"
5
```

Five calls: one in each of the four catch handlers (error, timeout, blocked, ok) plus the recovery function definition itself.

Evidence after fix: Gateway starts and runs normally. Terminal output from the compiled production bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "lifecycle.*completion failed" dist/subagent-registry-1ZF808Hw.js
1850:log.warn("lifecycle error completion failed"...
1884:log.warn("lifecycle timeout completion failed"...
2320:log.warn("lifecycle blocked completion failed"...
2355:log.warn("lifecycle ok completion failed"...
```

All four completion branches now have branch-aware catch recovery. The blocked branch retries with `status: "error"`, the ok branch retries with `status: "ok"`, matching the existing timer-path pattern.

Observed result after fix: Gateway operational with patched subagent lifecycle handlers. All four completion paths (error, timeout, blocked, ok) schedule `scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery` with branch-correct params on failure. The outer catch remains as a safety net for unexpected errors in the non-async setup code.

What was not tested: Intentionally crashing `completeSubagentRun` in a live gateway to observe each branch's catch-and-recover path. The recovery mechanism is exercised through the regression test which verifies error state preservation.

commands.author: Takhoffman

target.title: fix(agents/harness): route CLI backend aliases through PI for in-process compaction [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

`selectAgentHarnessDecision` in `src/agents/harness/selection.ts` treated `agentRuntime.id` strictly as a plugin harness id. CLI backend aliases (`claude-cli`, `google-gemini-cli`) are documented-valid runtime ids per the schema (see `runtime-schema.ts`: *"a registered plugin harness id such as 'codex', or a supported CLI backend alias such as 'claude-cli'"*), but they are not plugin harnesses — they are CLI backends dispatched at the request layer by `runCliAgent` in `attempt-execution.ts` and `agent-runner.runtime.ts`.

Normal turns never hit the harness selector for these runtimes because the request layer short-circuits on `isCliProvider` first. But **in-process consumers that call `selectAgentHarness` directly** — most importantly `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, used by both `/compact` and the preflight memory-flush path in `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` — would throw `MissingAgentHarnessError("claude-cli")` for any agent whose model-scoped or provider-scoped `agentRuntime.id` was a CLI backend alias.

Once a session crosses the preflight compaction threshold, that throw fires on **every inbound turn across every channel** (Telegram, webchat, SMS, `/compact`), because `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` runs before every dispatch. The agent becomes unusable until compacted — and `/compact` itself can't compact, so recovery requires a config swap or surgical patch.

## Fix

Add a `cli_backend_routed_via_pi` branch to `selectAgentHarnessDecision` right next to the existing `implicit_plugin_unavailable_pi` (codex) fallback. When the runtime is a CLI backend alias, return the PI harness with `runtime: "pi"` stamped on the policy. The new branch is gated by `isCliRuntimeAlias()` from `model-runtime-aliases.ts`, so any future CLI backend added to `LEGACY_RUNTIME_MODEL_PROVIDER_ALIASES` is covered automatically.

This routes only the **in-process step** through PI:
- For compaction: PI does the transcript summarization. Auth still flows through the user's existing CLI OAuth via `resolveClaudeCliSyntheticAuth`.
- For normal turns: unaffected. They never reach this code path because `runAgentAttempt` in `attempt-execution.ts` and `agent-runner.runtime.ts` branch to `runCliAgent` on `isCliProvider` first.

The branch mirrors the existing codex-implicit fallback shape exactly: same `buildSelectionDecision`, same `runtime: "pi"` policy override, same candidate list. No new architecture.

## Why not a CLI-native compaction path?

The CLI backend declares `reseedFromRawTranscriptWhenUncompacted: true` for the claude-cli case (see `cli-backend.ts`), which is the binary's own internal context management — not something the harness layer can call as "summarize this transcript please." Building a CLI-native compaction backend would be a much larger architectural change. PI fallback is the smallest fix consistent with how this function already handles "this runtime can't compact in-process" cases (codex implicit).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: With model-scoped `agentRuntime.id = "claude-cli"`, `selectAgentHarnessDecision` threw `MissingAgentHarnessError("claude-cli")` for every call. This blocked `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, so once a session crossed the preflight-compaction threshold, every inbound turn on every channel (Telegram, webchat, SMS, `/compact`) failed before reaching the request-layer CLI dispatch. User-visible symptom: an unresponsive bot, with `Requested agent harness "claude-cli" is not registered.` repeating in the gateway log.

- **Real environment tested**: macOS, Apple Silicon, OpenClaw 2026.5.19 installed fresh 2 days ago. Single agent (`main`) with `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` as primary model and model-scoped `agentRuntime: { id: "claude-cli" }`. Claude CLI binary at `~/.local/bin/claude`, authenticated via the `anthropic:claude-cli` OAuth profile. Telegram bot channel active. Session at 151% of context (1.6m / 1.0m) when the bug surfaced — preflight compaction firing on every inbound turn.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Applied the semantic equivalent of this PR's patch to the locally installed bundled selector (`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/selection-*.js`). The two-string-literal version of the source change in this PR — added the `cli_backend_routed_via_pi` branch in `selectAgentHarnessDecision`.
  2. `openclaw gateway restart` — new gateway PID came up, listening on configured port.
  3. Sent a regular message via Telegram to my running bot.
  4. Issued `/compact` in the same Telegram thread.
  5. Tailed `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-$(date +%Y-%m-%d).log` filtered for `MissingAgentHarnessError`, `agent/cli-backend`, and `provider=claude-cli` lines during steps 3–4.

- **Evidence after fix**:
  - **Telegram screenshot** (cropped, redacted) showing the bot's normal reply + `/compact` completing on the same channel that was throwing on every turn before the patch: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8a97397e-22e3-47b9-93e4-4b4d2e353727 (re-attached in my proof comment below for in-thread display).
  - **Before/after gateway log excerpt** (sensitive identifiers redacted, timestamps stripped): https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28105459/proof-logs.txt — attached in my proof comment.
  - Inline excerpt from the before-fix log (sensitive fields scrubbed; 5 of 24 occurrences across `lane=main` and `lane=session:agent:main:telegram:direct:<redacted>`):

    ```
    lane task error: lane=main durationMs=<redacted> error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."
    lane task error: lane=session:agent:main:telegram:direct:<redacted> durationMs=<redacted> error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."
    ```

  - Inline excerpt from the after-fix tail (same scrubbing; representative — every post-restart Telegram turn shows the same `agent/cli-backend` activity):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli model=sonnet promptChars=<redacted> trigger=user useResume=true session=present resumeSession=<redacted> reuse=reusable historyPrompt=present
    claude live session reuse: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6
    claude live session turn: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6 durationMs=<redacted> rawLines=<redacted>
    ```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Telegram normal turn replied successfully (the bot answered the prompt I gave it instead of failing the lane).
  - `/compact` returned `Compacted • Context 37k/1.0m (3%)` as a Telegram system message — context dropped from 151% to 3%.
  - Zero `MissingAgentHarnessError` lines in the post-restart filtered tail.
  - Every post-restart Telegram turn logged `provider=claude-cli` from the `agent/cli-backend` subsystem and emitted `claude live session reuse` / `claude live session turn` events. This confirms the request-layer CLI dispatch (`runCliAgent` → `claude` binary subprocess) is unchanged — normal turns still flow through the user's `claude` binary, not the PI fallback added by this patch. The PI fallback is only reached by `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, which is the intended scope.

- **What was not tested**: `google-gemini-cli` runtime end-to-end (no Gemini CLI installation available); covered by unit test in this PR only. Full `pnpm test` matrix beyond the targeted `selection.test.ts` file — happy to run on request. The bundled-JS hot-patch on my local install is semantically equivalent to this PR's source change (uses hardcoded `claude-cli`/`google-gemini-cli` literals because importing into bundled output is impractical; the source PR uses `isCliRuntimeAlias()`).

## Tests added

- `routes CLI backend aliases to the PI harness so in-process compaction works` — verifies `selectAgentHarness` returns the PI harness for both `claude-cli` and `google-gemini-cli` runtimes.
- `lets maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession proceed for claude-cli model runs via PI` — direct regression test for the user-facing bug: the compaction entrypoint resolves without throwing `MissingAgentHarnessError` for a `claude-cli`-aliased anthropic model.

## Related

- Same-shape-of-bug PR: #72499 (helper paths bypassing CLI dispatch — compaction was missed there)
- Issue with the same error message but different root cause (auth migration gap): #84604
- Issue that pointed me at this area: #57326

## AI-assisted disclosure

This patch was authored end-to-end via Claude Code (claude-opus-4-7). The PR author (me) ran the test suite and repo check personally, validated the bundled-JS equivalent of the patch on the live install that originally exhibited the bug (log evidence above), and read every line before pushing. The original bug surfaced on my own machine — the agent's job was to localize the throw site, design the minimum-viable fix consistent with existing patterns (mirroring the codex-implicit fallback), and write the regression tests.

target.title: fix(sessions): honor explicit default model pins

## Summary
- Persist explicit user selections of the configured default model as exact session model pins instead of clearing them as if they were unpinned defaults.
- Keep `/model default`, `session_status(model=default)`, and `sessions.patch model:null` as the explicit clear/unpin paths.
- Carry the exact selection through reply, queued-run, agent-command, reset, status, and sessions.patch paths so channel/parent/config fallback policy cannot silently replace user intent.

## Real behavior proof

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling into explicit `setup` and `request` phases in `src/media-understanding/image.ts`
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency from the request budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- document that `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` remains a per-request / per-phase timeout rather than a shared `setup + request` wall-clock budget
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: fix(codex): disable Codex built-in personality for native threads
target.body:
## Summary

This PR disables Codex's built-in personality layer for OpenClaw native Codex app-server threads.

OpenClaw still uses the native Codex app-server lane for Codex-owned base instructions, model behavior, tool/runtime behavior, and configured project-doc behavior. The change is specifically about the Codex `personality` field: OpenClaw now starts and resumes native Codex threads with `personality: "none"`.

## Why this is needed

OpenClaw agents can have their own workspace identity, behavior, and personality instructions, loaded through OpenClaw workspace context. Those instructions are expected to be authoritative for the agent.
Native Codex app-server runs currently receive Codex base instructions that include a default personality layer unless the `personality` request field is explicitly set. Because those instructions are part of Codex's base prompt, they sit at a very high priority. That creates a prompt conflict when an OpenClaw agent also has its own identity/personality instructions.

The practical failure mode is that the model sees two competing personality sources:

- Codex's built-in default personality from the native base instructions
- OpenClaw's workspace-defined agent personality and identity

Since the Codex personality is injected at a higher-priority prompt layer, any conflict weakens or overrides the OpenClaw-defined agent behavior. That is bad for agent consistency and especially bad for users who intentionally define strong workspace identity/personality instructions.

OpenClaw should keep Codex-native runtime behavior without also inheriting Codex's default persona. The runtime should provide execution semantics; the OpenClaw workspace should define the agent.

## How this is solved

This PR sets `personality: "none"` on both native Codex app-server request paths:
- `thread/start`
- `thread/resume`

That makes the behavior explicit and stable for newly started threads and resumed threads.

The implementation is intentionally small. It does not add a new OpenClaw configuration option, because the desired OpenClaw behavior is not another personality selector. OpenClaw already has its own prompt/personality surfaces. The correct default for native Codex integration is to disable Codex's built-in personality layer and let OpenClaw's workspace instructions remain authoritative.

## Documentation

The docs are updated to make the boundary clear:

- Native Codex still keeps Codex-owned base/model/project-doc behavior.
- OpenClaw disables Codex's built-in personality for native threads.
- OpenClaw workspace personality and identity instructions remain the intended authority for agent behavior.
## Testing

Verified with focused Codex app-server tests:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts`

Also verified formatting / diff hygiene:

- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Before the fix, I checked the factual base instructions provided to the codex harness. They are observable in codex sessions jsonl file, so I executed the following: `head -c 3000 ~/.openclaw/agents/home/agent/codex-home/sessions/2026/05/24/rollout-2026-05-24T02-50-40-019e573f-832f-7532-a43f-33a3ad49ec35.jsonl` (this is a session file before the fix), and got the following reply:

```text
{"timestamp":"2026-05-23T23:50:40.773Z","type":"session_meta","payload":{"id":"019e573f-832f-7532-a43f-33a3ad49ec35","timestamp":"2026-05-23T23:50:40.693Z","cwd":"/home/beru/.openclaw/workspace","originator":"openclaw","cli_version":"0.132.0","source":"vscode","model_provider":"openai","base_instructions":{"text":"You are Codex, a coding agent based on GPT-5. You and the user share one workspace, and your job is to collaborate with them until their goal is genuinely handled.\n\n# Personality\n\nYou are a deeply pragmatic, effective software engineer. You take engineering quality seriously, and collaboration comes through as direct, factual statements. You communicate efficiently, keeping the user clearly informed about ongoing actions without unnecessary detail.\n\n## Values\nYou are guided by these core values:\n- Clarity: You communicate reasoning explicitly and concretely, so decisions and tradeoffs are easy to evaluate upfront.\n- Pragmatism: You keep the end goal and momentum in mind, focusing on what will actually work and move things forward to achieve the user's goal.\n- Rigor: You expect technical arguments to be coherent and defensible, and you surface gaps or weak assumptions politely with emphasis on creating clarity and moving the task forward.\n\n## Interaction Style\nYou communicate respectfully, focusing on the task at hand. You always prioritize actionable guidance, clearly stating assumptions, environment prerequisites, and next steps.\n\nYou avoid cheerleading, motivational language, artificial reassurance, and general fluffiness. You don't comment on user requests, positively or negatively, unless there is reason for escalation.\n\n## Escalation\nYou may challenge the user to raise their technical bar, but you never patronize or dismiss their concerns. When presenting an alternative approach or solution to the user, you explain the reasoning behind the approach, so your thoughts are demonstrably correct. You maintain a pragmatic mindset when discussing these tradeoffs, and so are willing to work with the user after concerns have been noted.\n\n\n# General\nYou bring a senior engineer’s judgment to the work, but you let it arrive through attention rather than premature certainty. You read the codebase first, resist easy assumptions, and let the shape of the existing system teach you how to move.\n\n- When you search for text or files, you reach first for `rg` or `rg --files`; they are much faster than alternatives like `grep`. If `rg` is unavailable, you use the next best tool without fuss.\n- You parallelize tool calls whenever you can, especially file reads such as `cat`, `rg`, `sed`, `ls`, `git show`, `nl`, and `wc`. You use `multi_tool_use.parallel` for that parallelism, and only that. Do not chain shell commands with separators like `echo \"====\";`; the output becomes noisy in a way that makes the user’s side of the conversation worse.\n\n## Engineering judgment\n\nWhen the user leaves implementation details open, you
```

As it is observed here in this fragment, the base instructions contain personality section.

After the fix, I checked the factual base instructions provided to the codex harness with the following: `head -c 3000 ~/.openclaw/agents/home/agent/codex-home/sessions/2026/05/24/rollout-2026-05-24T04-01-02-019e577f-eca3-7b93-abd2-df28de13d4e1.jsonl` (this is a session file after the fix), and got the following reply:

```text
{"timestamp":"2026-05-24T01:01:02.328Z","type":"session_meta","payload":{"id":"019e577f-eca3-7b93-abd2-df28de13d4e1","timestamp":"2026-05-24T01:01:02.017Z","cwd":"/home/beru/.openclaw/workspace","originator":"openclaw","cli_version":"0.132.0","source":"vscode","model_provider":"openai","base_instructions":{"text":"You are Codex, a coding agent based on GPT-5. You and the user share one workspace, and your job is to collaborate with them until their goal is genuinely handled.\n\n\n\n# General\nYou bring a senior engineer’s judgment to the work, but you let it arrive through attention rather than premature certainty. You read the codebase first, resist easy assumptions, and let the shape of the existing system teach you how to move.\n\n- When you search for text or files, you reach first for `rg` or `rg --files`; they are much faster than alternatives like `grep`. If `rg` is unavailable, you use the next best tool without fuss.\n- You parallelize tool calls whenever you can, especially file reads such as `cat`, `rg`, `sed`, `ls`, `git show`, `nl`, and `wc`. You use `multi_tool_use.parallel` for that parallelism, and only that. Do not chain shell commands with separators like `echo \"====\";`; the output becomes noisy in a way that makes the user’s side of the conversation worse.\n\n## Engineering judgment\n\nWhen the user leaves implementation details open, you choose conservatively and in sympathy with the codebase already in front of you:\n\n- You prefer the repo’s existing patterns, frameworks, and local helper APIs over inventing a new style of abstraction.\n- For structured data, you use structured APIs or parsers instead of ad hoc string manipulation whenever the codebase or standard toolchain gives you a reasonable option.\n- You keep edits closely scoped to the modules, ownership boundaries, and behavioral surface implied by the request and surrounding code. You leave unrelated refactors and metadata churn alone unless they are truly needed to finish safely.\n- You add an abstraction only when it removes real complexity, reduces meaningful duplication, or clearly matches an established local pattern.\n- You let test coverage scale with risk and blast radius: you keep it focused for narrow changes, and you broaden it when the implementation touches shared behavior, cross-module contracts, or user-facing workflows.\n\n## Frontend guidance\n\nYou follow these instructions when building applications with a frontend experience:\n\n### Build with empathy\n- Match the app's domain, audience, and existing design system. Make common workflows complete, efficient, and natural.\n- Operational tools should be restrained, dense, and scannable. Games and playful apps can be expressive, animated, and illustrative.\n\n### Design instructions\n- Build the usable app, site, game, or tool as the first screen. Do not default to a marketing page unless a landing page is actually requested.\n- Use familiar controls: icons for tools, swatches for colors, se
```

As it is observed here in this fragment, the base instructions no longer have the personality section, and the first section after the preamble is `# General`. That section was present before the fix, but it followed the `# Personality` section that we removed with this fix.

target.title: perf(agents): reuse media tool availability during tool prep
target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for the image tool instead of resolving image availability separately.
- Let image/video/music generation tool factories skip their registration-time availability scan when createOpenClawTools already resolved availability.
- Use the same effective media tool config for the plan and constructed media tools.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`, Node 25.9.0 through repo `pnpm exec tsx`; warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a real local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; wrapped with `/usr/bin/time -l`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the previous same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50 (~45%). The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero. Remaining warm prep cost is mostly plugin-tools (~73ms p50) and image/media availability planning (~30ms p50).

What was not tested: Full end-to-end provider media generation was not run; this PR only changes registration-time availability reuse, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain covered by targeted tests.

## Verification
- pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core
- pnpm build
- pnpm check:changed --staged

## Summary
- add a shared scoped mention-pattern policy for global, agent, and provider/channel overrides

- document raw regex-body syntax and regenerate config/help metadata

## Notes

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Scoped mention-pattern policy now resolves the effective provider/agent/conversation policy so configured regex mentions are enabled only for explicitly allowed conversations in deny mode and remain disabled for denied or unlisted conversations. Latest follow-up rebased onto current `origin/main`, resolved refreshed generated config/plugin SDK baseline conflicts, and reran the exact-head behavior proof.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR checkout on host `patrick`, branch `feature/mention-pattern-policy-clean`, head `c4a2be36ad7dd3a6d544a4dab693efb978f18494`, running the real source resolver, Telegram group peer id helper, mention matcher, and inbound mention decision code through Node/tsx against redacted OpenClaw-style config/message facts. This does not exercise live external delivery.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `cd /home/patrick/projects/openclaw-mention-pattern-policy && HEAD_SHA=c4a2be36ad7dd3a6d544a4dab693efb978f18494 HEAD_SHORT=c4a2be36ad node --import tsx /tmp/pr70864-proof-02d093.mjs`
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real source smoke:

```text
OpenClaw scoped mention policy Telegram account media pre-download proof (c4a2be36ad, c4a2be36ad7dd3a6d544a4dab693efb978f18494)
{"topic":7,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:7","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":true,"wasMentioned":true,"shouldSkip":false,"skipReason":null}
```
