commands.author: rendrag-git

target.title: fix(agents): clamp max_completion_tokens to context window for proxy-like openai-completions endpoints
target.body:
## Summary

Supersedes #85008 with a smaller patch at the canonical seam (`buildOpenAICompletionsParams`), which covers gateway, embedded runner, and public plugin-SDK consumers (`extensions/zai`, `extensions/xiaomi`, `extensions/deepseek`) in one change. Fixes #83086.

- **Problem**: OpenClaw can emit `max_completion_tokens >= model.contextWindow` on Chat Completions requests. Strict OpenAI-compatible servers (vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, LM Studio, DashScope, GLM, Kimi) reject this with HTTP 400: `This model's maximum context length is N tokens. However, you requested N output tokens and your prompt contains ...`. Wildcard-discovered vLLM models in particular default to very large `maxTokens`, hitting this immediately.
- **Why it matters**: Local vLLM and other strict OpenAI-compat users see every prompt fail with HTTP 400 before the model is ever called. Issue #83086 reports the same against StepFun step-router-v1 with `context=262144`.
- **What changed**: After `resolveOpenAICompletionsMaxTokens`, clamp the resolved value to `contextWindow - 1` when `compatDetection.capabilities.usesExplicitProxyLikeEndpoint` is true and `effectiveMaxTokens >= contextWindow`. 12 lines added in `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts`, plus 3 unit tests.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: Native OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, OpenRouter — byte-identical defaults. The clamp is gated on proxy-like endpoint detection only. No subtracting of input tokens (deliberately — see "Why not the heuristic" below). No new helper module. No new stream wrapper. Builder seam only.

## Why a new fix instead of extending #85008

- **Canonical seam**: `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` is the single function that produces the wire payload. Fixing here covers every caller in one place.
- **#85008's interceptor at `pi-embedded-runner/extra-params.ts`** only covers traffic through the embedded runner. Gateway `openclaw infer model run` and external plugin-SDK consumers don't benefit.
- **No char→token heuristic**: #85008 uses `chars/4 * 1.2` to estimate input tokens. That's wrong by 50%+ for JSON tool schemas, Chinese, base64. Wrong-low still 400s; wrong-high needlessly truncates output. This PR instead caps the *requested output budget* to under the context window; the server still enforces `prompt + output ≤ context` itself. The misconfig case (output budget >= entire window) is what #83086 actually reports.
- **Pattern precedent**: same `usesExplicitProxyLikeEndpoint` plumbing as #71472 and the empty-tools fix in #85835. Drops into existing compat infrastructure with no new file or wrapper.
- **Diff size**: 12 LOC vs ~250 LOC across 3 files + 14 new `vi.mock()` blocks in #85008's test surface.

## Why not the full input-aware clamp (for now)

Doing it accurately requires either provider-specific tokenization or a heuristic. OpenClaw already computes `systemPromptChars + historyTextChars + promptChars` at the `[context-diag]` pre-prompt phase — a follow-up could thread that into the builder for a real input-aware clamp without a fresh estimator. That's worth doing on top of this PR but doesn't need to land together.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83086
- Supersedes #85008 (different seam; this PR fixes the canonical builder with no heuristic estimator)
- Related #85835 (same `usesExplicitProxyLikeEndpoint` compat plumbing, same builder, empty-tools fix)
- Related #74738 (preserved `supportsModelTools` gate)
- Related #71472 (same proxy-like detection pattern)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: vLLM rejects OpenClaw OpenAI-compatible chat completions when `max_completion_tokens >= max_model_len`.
- **Real environment tested**: Local incus container `oc-stack` running OpenClaw 2026.5.18 with this patch (and the empty-tools fix from #85835) applied to the live `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/openai-transport-stream-*.js` bundle on the pmg gateway (port 18789). vLLM router at `http://10.68.198.1:31080/v1` serving `qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4` with `max_model_len=131072` initially, later raised to `237568` after a vLLM rollout. Model registered as `vllm/qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`. Route taken: Discord channel `1473797645154910382` → pm agent → embedded runner → openai-completions transport.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Sent ordinary Discord messages in the pm channel. With `model.maxTokens` set high (200000+) and `contextWindow=131072`, the request hits the clamp branch (`effectiveMaxTokens >= contextWindow`) and `params.max_completion_tokens` becomes `131071`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs from the pmg gateway showing the live `openclaw infer` + Discord trace:

  ```text
  [model-fetch] start provider=vllm api=openai-completions
    model=qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 method=POST
    url=http://10.68.198.1:31080/v1/chat/completions
  [model-fetch] response provider=vllm
    model=qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 status=200 elapsedMs=122
    contentType=text/event-stream; charset=utf-8
  embedded run agent end: runId=... isError=false
  textPreview="Hey! Testing received 👋 How can I help you today?"
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: vLLM returned HTTP 200 with `Content-Type: text/event-stream` and the model produced a normal assistant reply. Before the patch, the same path returned HTTP 400 with `This model's maximum context length is 131072 tokens. However, you requested 131072 output tokens and your prompt contains ...`.
- **What was not tested**: StepFun step-router-v1 specifically (no account); the full input-token-aware clamp (deliberately deferred — see PR summary).
- **Before evidence**: vLLM access log returning `HTTP 400 ... max_completion_tokens=131072 cannot be greater than max_model_len=131072` on the unpatched gateway, captured during diagnostic write-up at `docs/diagnostics/vllm-openclaw-payload-compat-2026-05-23.md` (also produced in this conversation).

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` sets `max_completion_tokens` from `resolveOpenAICompletionsMaxTokens(model, options)` with no awareness of `model.contextWindow`. When `model.maxTokens >= model.contextWindow` (common for wildcard-discovered vLLM models, also reproducible with StepFun per #83086), the wire request asks the server for an output budget that equals or exceeds the entire context. Strict servers reject with HTTP 400; lenient servers silently truncate or accept.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: No test exercised `model.maxTokens >= model.contextWindow` on a proxy-like endpoint.
- **Contributing context**: Wildcard discovery in `models.providers.<vllm>.models = []` populates per-model `maxTokens` from provider defaults that can match `max_model_len` and exceed conservative output budgets.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file**: `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- **Scenarios locked in by the new tests in this PR**:
  1. Proxy-like endpoint + `maxTokens >= contextWindow` → `max_completion_tokens` clamped to `contextWindow - 1`.
  2. Proxy-like endpoint + `maxTokens < contextWindow` → `max_completion_tokens` unchanged.
  3. Native OpenAI endpoint + `maxTokens >= contextWindow` → `max_completion_tokens` unchanged (existing behavior preserved).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

For OpenAI-compatible endpoints routed through a non-OpenAI base URL (vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, LM Studio, DashScope, GLM, Kimi, and similar): outbound `max_completion_tokens` is now capped to `contextWindow - 1` when the configured `maxTokens` would otherwise meet or exceed the context window. Native OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, and OpenRouter behavior is byte-identical.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
maxTokens = 200000, contextWindow = 131072  -->  max_completion_tokens: 200000  -->  strict server HTTP 400

After (proxy-like endpoint only):
maxTokens = 200000, contextWindow = 131072  -->  max_completion_tokens: 131071  -->  HTTP 200
maxTokens =   8192, contextWindow = 131072  -->  max_completion_tokens:   8192  -->  unchanged
Native OpenAI: unchanged regardless of inputs.
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` (only payload field value bound on existing calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- **OS**: Ubuntu 22.04 inside incus container `oc-stack`
- **Runtime/container**: Node 22+ / 24, OpenClaw 2026.5.18 + this patch
- **Model/provider**: vLLM serving `qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`, `max_model_len=131072` (later raised to `237568`); OpenClaw model id `vllm/qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`
- **Integration/channel**: Discord channel bound to the pm agent via existing routing
- **Relevant config (redacted)**: vLLM router `http://10.68.198.1:31080/v1` (Bearer token redacted); model catalog entry registers contextWindow + maxTokens explicitly.

### Steps

1. Configure a vLLM provider on a proxy-like base URL with a model entry where `maxTokens >= contextWindow`.
2. Bind a Discord channel to an agent using that model.
3. Send a message.

### Expected

- Gateway log shows `[model-fetch] response status=200`. Outbound `max_completion_tokens` is clamped to `contextWindow - 1`.

### Actual

- Matches expected.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: End-to-end Discord → pm agent → embedded runner → openai-completions transport against live vLLM with `maxTokens > contextWindow`; vLLM accepts the request and the model replies normally.
- **Edge cases checked**: `maxTokens < contextWindow` is unchanged (sanity); native OpenAI byte-identical (unit test).
- **What I did not verify**: Native OpenAI live with `maxTokens > contextWindow`; StepFun step-router-v1 from #83086 (no account).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- **Backward compatible?** `Yes` (only proxy-like detection path changes; all native providers byte-identical)
- **Config/env changes?** `No`
- **Migration needed?** `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A strict OpenAI-compatible provider expects `max_completion_tokens == contextWindow` for some reason.
  - **Mitigation**: Clamping to `contextWindow - 1` still allows up to almost the full window for output. Any prompt requires at least one token, so `output ≤ contextWindow - 1` is always a stricter upper bound than the server's own requirement.
- **Risk**: Users had set `maxTokens = contextWindow` intentionally and now see 1 less token of output.
  - **Mitigation**: This is precisely the misconfig that #83086 reports as broken. The clamp turns a guaranteed-failure into a guaranteed-success with negligible output loss.
- **Risk**: This doesn't subtract input tokens, so very long prompts can still 400.
  - **Mitigation**: Acknowledged in the PR summary; deliberate scope. Follow-up can thread OpenClaw's existing `[context-diag]` char counts into the builder for an input-aware clamp.

---

> AI-assisted PR. Investigation, full PR archaeology, patch authoring, and PR body produced with Claude (Opus 4.7). Real behavior proof is from my own local vLLM setup; I personally ran the live Discord call and inspected the outbound payload against redacted runtime logs from the pmg gateway.

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate the value as a string array, preserving explicit `[]` as an empty allowlist.
- Forward the allowlist through `SpawnSubagentParams` into the child `agent` Gateway request so the existing embedded-runner allowlist and tool construction path apply.
- Reject `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` because ACP does not use the embedded-runner tool construction path.
- Reject `toolsAllow` for CLI-backed native runs because CLI harnesses cannot enforce OpenClaw embedded-runner tool allowlists.
- Document the user-facing option and add an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
- Behavior or issue addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can now pass a narrow native subagent tool allowlist with `toolsAllow`; the value is exposed in the live tool schema and forwarded through the child Gateway `agent` path into the existing native embedded-runner allowlist filtering.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local OpenClaw source checkout from branch `sessions-spawn-tools-allow-pr2`.
- PR head tested: `09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: rebuilt dist with `pnpm build`, then used an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, loopback token-auth Gateway, temp workspace, temp mock OpenAI `/v1/responses` server, and no live remote model/provider call. The same child Gateway `agent` path that `sessions_spawn` forwards into was run three times: without `toolsAllow`, with `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`, and with `toolsAllow: []`.
- Evidence after fix: the mock OpenAI server captured the actual `tools` array sent by the embedded runner. Baseline sent 21 tools; the allowlisted run sent only `exec` and `read`; the explicit empty allowlist run sent 0 tools. Current-head focused tests also prove CLI-backed native runs reject `toolsAllow` before dispatch for both `["read"]` and `[]`, and explicit empty embedded `toolsAllow` uses the minimal prompt path without the skills catalog.

```json
{
  "head": "09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385",
  "gatewayPort": 41167,
  "mockOpenAiBaseUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:41168/v1",
  "baseline": {
    "label": "baseline",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-baseline-6262660c-a744-4330-972c-56051148dc92",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 2296,
    "toolCount": 21,
    "toolNames": [
      "edit",
      "exec",
      "image",
      "image_generate",
      "memory_get",
      "memory_search",
      "music_generate",
      "process",
      "read",
      "session_status",
      "sessions_history",
      "sessions_list",
      "sessions_send",
      "sessions_spawn",
      "sessions_yield",
      "subagents",
      "update_plan",
      "video_generate",
      "web_fetch",
      "web_search",
      "write"
    ]
  },
  "allowReadExec": {
    "label": "allow-read-exec",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-allow-read-exec-42d95c53-529a-460e-ba17-bab5ff57143b",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 440,
    "toolCount": 2,
    "toolNames": [
      "exec",
      "read"
    ]
  },
  "emptyAllow": {
    "label": "empty-allow",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-empty-allow-769c291b-1f33-4da9-afb3-644c0a1f3474",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 356,
    "toolCount": 0,
    "toolNames": []
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: final-head embedded native subagent runs enforce `toolsAllow` before the request reaches the model transport, and explicit `[]` disables tools instead of falling back to unrestricted defaults. CLI-backed native runs fail closed instead of silently ignoring the allowlist.
- Before evidence: without this PR, `sessions_spawn` had no `toolsAllow` schema entry and the spawned child Gateway `agent` request could not carry a per-spawn allowlist.
- What was not tested: a live remote model-backed spawned child run. The proof intentionally uses a mock OpenAI server to avoid real provider calls while still exercising the Gateway and embedded-runner path.

## Supplemental local timing note
The proof run also recorded local mock wall time: baseline `2296ms`, allowlisted `440ms`, empty allowlist `356ms`. This is included only as a local mock timing signal from one isolated run, not as a formal benchmark or guaranteed provider/model speedup. The primary value of this PR is correctness/control-plane narrowing; any latency win depends on tool set, runtime config, and provider behavior.

## Root Cause (if applicable)
N/A. This is additive feature plumbing over an existing embedded-runner allowlist mechanism.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`
- Scenario the tests lock in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects ACP runtime usage, forwards through Gateway `agent`, and reaches embedded runs started by the command runner.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: this PR connects an existing embedded-runner option to `sessions_spawn`; focused tests cover the schema, validation, and forwarding contract without requiring live model behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow the spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when the field is omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects the option with a clear input error.

## Diagram (if applicable)
N/A.

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. ACP rejects the option because it does not use this embedded tool construction path.

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS 26.2
- Runtime/container: Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local source checkout
- Model/provider: mock OpenAI `/v1/responses`; no live remote model call
- Integration/channel: local loopback Gateway `agent` RPC and `agent.wait`
- Relevant config: temp workspace, temp state dir, token-auth loopback Gateway, mock provider `mock-openai/gpt-pr2-tools`

### Commands and checks
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts` passed (`5 files`, `186 tests`, 2 Vitest shards).
- `pnpm tsgo:core` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed (`0 warnings and 0 errors`).
- `git diff --check` passed before the docs/changelog commit.
- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD` passed after the CLI fail-closed review-fix commit.
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check` printed `Prompt snapshots are current (7 files)`; the process then hung after printing and was killed.
- `pnpm build` passed before the current-head Gateway/mock proof.
- Final-head Gateway/mock proof above passed on `09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385`.

### Expected
- Omitted `toolsAllow`: default native child tool set is unchanged.
- `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`: the child model request only sees `read` and `exec` tools.
- `toolsAllow: []`: the child model request sees no tools.

### Actual
- Omitted `toolsAllow`: 21 tools captured by mock OpenAI.
- `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`: 2 tools captured by mock OpenAI, exactly `exec` and `read`.
- `toolsAllow: []`: 0 tools captured by mock OpenAI.

## Evidence
- [x] Gateway-backed terminal capture
- [x] Mock transport request capture
- [x] Focused tests, typecheck, lint, whitespace check

## Human Verification (required)
- Verified scenarios: schema exposure, native forwarding path, Gateway forwarding path, embedded command runner propagation, strict validation tests, ACP rejection tests, CLI-backed native fail-closed behavior for non-empty and empty allowlists, embedded empty-allowlist minimal prompt behavior, formatter/typecheck/lint/whitespace checks, final-head Gateway behavior proof.
- Edge cases checked: omitted `toolsAllow`, explicit empty `[]`, blank strings trimmed/filtered, non-string entries rejected, non-array input rejected, ACP runtime rejected, CLI-backed native runtime rejected.
- What you did **not** verify: a live provider-backed spawned child run.

## Review Conversations
- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect CLI-backed native runs.
  - Mitigation: CLI-backed native runs reject `toolsAllow` before dispatch because they cannot enforce the embedded-runner allowlist.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses the existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

target.title: fix: preflight malformed OpenShell exec commands
target.body:
Fixes #72373.

## Summary
- Add a shared SSH exec command preflight before remote `/bin/sh -c` execution.
- Reject unresolved generated placeholders like `<workflow-id>` and common malformed shell input such as unclosed quotes, trailing escapes, unterminated command substitutions, and unterminated here-docs.
- Preserve legitimate shell syntax including redirection, compact redirection, here-doc bodies, process substitution, arithmetic expansion, quoted placeholder-looking text, and comments.
- Build remote exec commands before opening SSH/OpenShell sessions so malformed input fails before session setup.

This follows ClawSweeper's suggested fix shape: keep the existing loop-detection behavior and add the remaining focused validator at the shared SSH/OpenShell exec boundary.

## Proof
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sandbox/ssh.test.ts extensions/openshell/src/openshell-core.test.ts -t "rejects malformed generated exec commands|allows shell features and quoted placeholder-looking text|uses the shared SSH exec command preflight|rejects malformed exec commands before opening"` -> 22 passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/sandbox/ssh.ts src/agents/sandbox/ssh.test.ts src/agents/sandbox/ssh-backend.ts src/agents/sandbox.ts src/plugin-sdk/sandbox.ts extensions/openshell/src/backend.ts extensions/openshell/src/cli.ts extensions/openshell/src/openshell-core.test.ts` -> passed
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` -> passed
- `git diff --check` -> passed

## Local Test Note
The broader requested Windows run of `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sandbox/ssh.test.ts extensions/openshell/src/openshell-core.test.ts` still fails on existing Windows-only assumptions unrelated to this patch: path slicing around `config` and symlink creation permissions. The new preflight-focused cases pass.

target.title: [perf] improve reply send performance
target.body:
## Summary
- improve reply latency by avoiding dispatcher-idle waits on every direct block reply callback
- flush pending block delivery only before subsequent tool/progress events, preserving block-before-tool ordering
- keep ACP direct block replies non-blocking while preserving visible reply chronology
- schedule source reply transcript mirroring after successful `message.action` sends without blocking the gateway response
- serialize async source transcript mirrors per session so same-session transcript chronology is preserved
- send Telegram direct-message typing once access has passed, before later context/session construction can delay the indicator, while avoiding a duplicate immediate cue

## Root Cause
Recent reply-path changes moved delivery bookkeeping into hot paths for lightweight replies: block callbacks waited for dispatcher idle, and `message.action` waited for transcript mirroring after platform delivery. Under concurrent embedded runs, cron work, or slow channel/transcript I/O, this amplified reply latency and event-loop starvation. The 36-54h window also includes Telegram dispatch replay dedupe changes that added work before the existing typing cue path.

## Impact
Simple visible replies can return sooner without compromising user-visible send order. The existing block-before-tool/progress ordering guarantee is preserved by flushing only when later progress is about to be emitted. Transcript mirroring remains best-effort and still runs after successful source-conversation sends, with per-session ordering to avoid mirrored transcript reordering. Telegram direct typing starts earlier after authorization without sending an extra duplicate initial cue.

## Validation
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: Disable Chrome MCP telemetry watchdog by default
target.body:
## Summary

- Pass `--no-usage-statistics` when OpenClaw launches `chrome-devtools-mcp@latest`.
- Preserve explicit profile-level `mcpArgs` overrides for usage statistics.
- Add regression coverage for the default arg list, explicit opt-in, explicit opt-out dedupe, and custom-command launches.

## Motivation / root cause

`chrome-devtools-mcp` enables usage statistics by default and can spawn a detached watchdog process. OpenClaw closes the MCP SDK stdio transport, but that only owns the direct child process. Disabling usage statistics at launch prevents the detached telemetry watchdog from being created in the default OpenClaw browser automation path.

Refs #85721

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Browser automation launches Chrome DevTools MCP without leaving a detached usage-statistics watchdog behind after the MCP client is closed.

Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, Node 24.10.0, real `npx chrome-devtools-mcp@latest` process launched through `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` `StdioClientTransport`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a real MCP SDK `Client` with `StdioClientTransport({ command: "npx", args: buildChromeMcpArgs({ cdpUrl: "http://127.0.0.1:9" }) })`, called `client.connect()`, `client.listTools()`, then `client.close()`, waited 1.2s, and scanned `ps axo pid=,ppid=,command=` for `chrome-devtools-mcp` and `watchdog/main.js`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live console output from the real SDK stdio close/process-scan smoke:

```json
{
  "generatedArgs": [
    "-y",
    "chrome-devtools-mcp@latest",
    "--browserUrl",
    "http://127.0.0.1:9",
    "--no-usage-statistics",
    "--experimentalStructuredContent",
    "--experimental-page-id-routing"
  ],
  "transportPid": 14467,
  "remainingRelevantProcesses": [],
  "watchdogProcessesRemaining": 0
}
```

Observed result after fix: Live output shows `--no-usage-statistics` in the generated argv, `remainingRelevantProcesses: []`, and `watchdogProcessesRemaining: 0`; no `chrome-devtools-mcp` or telemetry watchdog process remained after closing the SDK client.

What was not tested: I did not run a full Chrome browser UI workflow or a Linux remote/Testbox smoke in this pass.

## Regression tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/basic.existing-session.test.ts`

## Exact commands run

- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/basic.existing-session.test.ts`
- Real MCP SDK stdio close/process-scan smoke described above.

## User-visible behavior

Existing-session browser automation should no longer accumulate detached Chrome DevTools MCP telemetry watchdog processes during normal OpenClaw-managed MCP launches.

## Compatibility

Users who explicitly want upstream usage statistics can still pass a usage-statistics arg in `browser.profiles.*.mcpArgs`; explicit args override OpenClaw's default opt-out.

## Risks

Low. The change adds one documented Chrome DevTools MCP flag to the existing argv list and keeps profile-level custom args as separate argv entries.

target.title: perf(auto-reply): move memory flush off the user-visible reply path
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` (the near-threshold housekeeping
  embedded run) ran on the pre-reply path of `runReplyAgent`, so the
  user-visible reply turn could not start until that embedded LLM call
  finished.
- Fix: dispatch the housekeeping run from the reply orchestrator's
  `finally` block (after `replyOperation.complete()`), register the
  in-flight flush in a per-`sessionKey` map, and barrier on it from the
  next turn's preflight compaction before any compaction state is
  evaluated. The deferred dispatch builds a **flush-safe maintenance
  ReplyOperation** for each post-reply flush so the embedded runner's
  `attachBackend(...)` call does not get cancelled by the already-
  completed reply operation.
- Files: `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`,
  `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts`,
  `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.ts` (new
  `createMaintenanceReplyOperation` export), plus targeted unit tests.
- Out of scope: the flush body itself, the existing
  `memoryFlushCompactionCount` / `hasAlreadyFlushedForCurrentCompaction`
  dedup semantics, heartbeat / CLI / followup-runner paths, persisted
  session state format.

## Motivation

On near-threshold turns, the orchestrator's order was
`preflight compaction → runMemoryFlushIfNeeded (LLM call) → runAgentTurnWithFallback`.
The reply turn could not begin until the housekeeping embedded run
completed. Moving the housekeeping dispatch off the user-visible reply
startup path lets the reply turn start as soon as preflight compaction
has resolved, while still resolving any required flush before a later
compaction can proceed.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` was awaited inline before the
  reply turn. Its embedded run can itself trigger a compaction that
  mutates `followupRun.run.sessionId` and calls
  `replyOperation.updateSessionId(...)`. That coupling made it unsafe
  to simply run it concurrently with the reply turn; instead the
  dispatch is moved to *after* `replyOperation.complete()` (when
  `setPhase` / `updateSessionId` become no-ops by `ReplyOperation`'s own
  contract), so the flush path can update the active run/session during
  its internal compaction without racing the reply.
- The naive parallel approach is **not** used: the flush path can
  update the active run/session during compaction, so the change keeps
  flush execution serialized with session mutation rather than running
  it concurrently with the reply.
- Deferred-dispatch contract: forwarding the original
  `replyOperation` into `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` after
  `replyOperation.complete()` would cancel the embedded runner's
  `attachBackend(queueHandle)` call. `attachBackend` cancels any handle
  attached to a completed operation with reason `"superseded"`
  (see `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.ts:attachBackend`), so
  the deferred flush would short-circuit before its LLM call ever ran
  and `memoryFlushAt` / `memoryFlushCompactionCount` would stay stale
  on disk. The deferred dispatch builds a new flush-safe maintenance
  `ReplyOperation` (`createMaintenanceReplyOperation`) per call: it is
  not registered in `replyRunState`, has its own AbortController, and
  only cancels attached backends when the maintenance op itself is
  completed or aborted. Abort on the original reply's signal
  propagates one-way into the maintenance op so an explicit user
  cancellation still stops the flush.
- Barrier-timeout contract: the pre-compaction barrier
  `awaitPendingMemoryFlush` has a wait-cap (30 s default) so a hung
  flush cannot block subsequent turns forever. The wait-cap is made
  **terminal** by aborting the pending flush before the barrier
  returns: each registered pending entry carries an abort callback,
  which the orchestrator wires to `maintenanceReplyOperation.
  abortForRestart()`. On wait-cap timeout the barrier invokes the
  callback (cancelling the attached backend handle and aborting the
  embedded run's `abortSignal`) and then waits for the flush promise
  to settle. The post-abort wait is itself bounded by a 5 s default
  grace window (`POST_ABORT_GRACE_MS`): if the flush has not settled
  within that window (e.g. the abort callback threw, or the embedded
  runner ignored its `AbortSignal`), the barrier removes the pending
  entry from the registry and returns so subsequent turns are not
  blocked indefinitely. The abandoned flush may still complete later
  and mutate session state; that bounded risk is explicitly preferred
  over an unbounded availability hang. In practice the maintenance
  `ReplyOperation` short-circuits the embedded runner well inside the
  grace window, so the next preflight compaction proceeds against
  fresh session state.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: two behaviors.
  1. Near-threshold memory flush previously blocked the start of the
     user-visible reply turn. After this change the user-visible reply
     path begins before the housekeeping embedded run starts; the
     housekeeping run is dispatched after `replyOperation.complete()`
     and a later compaction cannot proceed past an unfinished required
     flush.
  2. **Completed-reply attachBackend cancellation path:**
     `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` forwards its `replyOperation` into
     `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, and the embedded runner calls
     `replyOperation.attachBackend(queueHandle)` (see
     `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts:3378`).
     `attachBackend` cancels any handle attached to a completed
     operation with reason `"superseded"`. Forwarding the
     already-completed reply operation into the deferred dispatch
     would therefore cancel the embedded runner's queue handle before
     any LLM call ran. This patch addresses that path by building a
     **flush-safe maintenance `ReplyOperation`** for each post-reply
     dispatch.

- Real environment tested: production `runReplyAgent` orchestrator run
  end-to-end via the committed regression tests under
  `src/auto-reply/reply/`. The source files this PR changes
  (`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`,
  `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts`, and the new
  `createMaintenanceReplyOperation` export in
  `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.ts`) execute unmodified.
  The substitutions are at the LLM-boundary modules
  (`pi-embedded.js`, `model-fallback.js`); the embedded LLM call is a
  controlled stub that **mirrors the real embedded runner by calling
  `params.replyOperation.attachBackend(handle)`** with a stub backend
  handle whose `cancel(reason)` records the cancellation reason. Both
  the production orchestrator wiring and the
  `createMaintenanceReplyOperation` body are exercised by the
  committed tests. The supplemental timing capture below also records
  `performance.now()` timestamps from a wrapped `ReplyOperation` for
  illustration.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  Reproducible by anyone (these tests ship with the PR):

  ```
  # Targeted regression tests covering the completed-reply
  # attachBackend cancellation path, the maintenance ReplyOperation
  # contract, the post-reply dispatch site in the orchestrator's
  # finally block, and the cross-turn barrier.
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.test.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts
  ```

  Supplemental wall-clock timing capture (used to record the
  `EVENT` ordering shown under "Evidence after fix" below). The
  capture runner is not committed in this PR; it exercises the
  production `runReplyAgent` with the LLM boundary stubbed. Every
  invariant the capture asserts (ordering and `attachBackend`
  non-cancellation) is also covered by the committed tests above,
  which any reviewer can run directly.

  ```
  # 1. With upstream/main copies of the two source files this PR
  #    changes, the capture runner records the BEFORE timeline:
  git checkout upstream/main -- \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts
  # 2. Restore PR HEAD copies to record the AFTER timeline:
  git checkout HEAD -- \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts
  ```

- Evidence after fix:

  Terminal capture from running the production reply orchestrator
  against `upstream/main` source files (BEFORE):

  ```
  EVENT [    5.37 ms] proof.start
  EVENT [  760.13 ms] reply.preflight_compacting.phase_set
  EVENT [  819.42 ms] reply.memory_flushing.phase_set
  EVENT [  869.51 ms] flush.embedded_run.started  trigger=memory backend_cancel=none
  EVENT [ 1069.77 ms] flush.embedded_run.completed
  EVENT [ 1075.69 ms] reply.running.phase_set
  EVENT [ 1133.50 ms] reply.agent_turn.invoked
  EVENT [ 1184.12 ms] reply.agent_turn.returning
  EVENT [ 1295.35 ms] reply.operation.complete

  OBSERVATION  flush.embedded_run.started (870 ms) < reply.operation.complete (1295 ms)
               → pre-reply (BASELINE) ordering
  OBSERVATION  embedded runner attachBackend: attached=true cancelReason=null
               (baseline attaches before complete, so no cancel — the
                cancellation regression only appears when the deferred
                dispatch reuses the original reply operation, which the
                fix in this PR avoids)
  ```

  Terminal capture from the same orchestrator code path against this
  PR's source files (AFTER):

  ```
  EVENT [    5.00 ms] proof.start
  EVENT [  738.63 ms] reply.preflight_compacting.phase_set
  EVENT [  747.38 ms] reply.running.phase_set
  EVENT [  849.48 ms] reply.agent_turn.invoked
  EVENT [  899.95 ms] reply.agent_turn.returning
  EVENT [ 1002.99 ms] reply.operation.complete
  EVENT [ 1051.31 ms] flush.embedded_run.started  trigger=memory backend_cancel=none
  EVENT [ 1252.03 ms] flush.embedded_run.completed

  PASS  reply.agent_turn.returning (900 ms) < flush.embedded_run.started (1051 ms)
  PASS  reply.operation.complete  (1003 ms) < flush.embedded_run.started (1051 ms)
  PASS  flush.embedded_run elapsed: 200.72 ms
  PASS  embedded runner attached backend handle, backend NOT cancelled (cancelReason=null)
  ```

  The final PASS line is the deferred-dispatch contract check:
  the proof's `runEmbeddedPiAgent` stub mirrors
  `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts:3378` by calling
  `params.replyOperation.attachBackend(handle)`. After the fix, the
  dispatched flush observes a non-completed maintenance ReplyOperation
  and the handle is stored rather than cancelled. Without the fix (the
  pre-fix HEAD), this stub would have recorded `cancelReason=superseded`.

  Cross-turn barrier capture from the same proof runner (PR HEAD, a
  second `runReplyAgent` call with a still-pending flush from turn 1):

  ```
  EVENT [ 1264.90 ms] turn2.preflight.start
  OBSERVE before release: reply agent turn invoked? false
  EVENT [ 1317.93 ms] reply.running.phase_set
  EVENT [ 1507.23 ms] reply.operation.complete
  EVENT [ 1552.49 ms] turn2.preflight.return

  PASS  reply agent turn NOT invoked while previous-turn flush was still pending
  PASS  reply agent turn invoked AFTER barrier resolved
  ```

- Observed result after fix:
  - The reply path no longer waits for near-threshold memory flush
    before beginning the user-visible response; any required flush is
    still resolved before a later compaction can proceed.
  - The runtime log above shows
    `flush.embedded_run.started` ordered **before**
    `reply.operation.complete` on baseline (870 ms vs 1295 ms) and
    **after** `reply.operation.complete` on PR HEAD
    (1051 ms vs 1003 ms).
  - The deferred dispatch passes a **non-completed maintenance
    ReplyOperation** into `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, so the embedded
    runner's `attachBackend(...)` call stores the handle rather than
    cancelling it with `superseded`. The proof's
    `runEmbeddedPiAgent` stub records `cancelReason=null` after the
    fix.
  - A subsequent turn's preflight compaction barriers on the
    previous turn's pending flush before invoking
    `compactEmbeddedPiSession`.

- What was not tested: live channel wall-clock TTFT against a real
  Anthropic / OpenAI / Bedrock model. The embedded LLM call is stubbed
  with a fixed 200 ms latency for the proof. Token / cost effect is
  not claimed as a measured number — this change moves the
  housekeeping embedded run off the user-visible reply startup path.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- The user-visible reply turn now starts before any near-threshold
  memory flush runs. Any required flush still runs after
  `replyOperation.complete()` and is awaited by the next turn's
  preflight compaction before any new compaction state mutation.
- Removed user-visible behavior: previously, if the housekeeping
  embedded run surfaced a visible error payload (rare; e.g., the
  housekeeping agent reported a target-path write restriction), the
  orchestrator returned that payload **in place of** the reply for the
  same turn. With the housekeeping run moved off the pre-reply path,
  that same-turn substitution is no longer possible. When the flush
  surfaces a visible error payload and the orchestrator (post-reply
  dispatch) does not supply an `onVisibleErrorPayloads` callback, the
  flush body forwards each dropped payload's text through
  `logVerbose(...)` so it is recorded in the gateway log rather than
  silently dropped. If maintainers prefer to retain visible surfacing,
  it can be added as a follow-up PR that emits a deferred channel
  notification after the flush settles.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[user message]
  -> preflight compaction
  -> ** memory flush embedded run (blocks here on threshold-crossing turns) **
  -> reply turn (runAgentTurnWithFallback)
  -> reply delivered

After:
[user message]
  -> preflight compaction
       -> await any pending memory flush from the previous turn (bounded timeout)
  -> reply turn (runAgentTurnWithFallback)
  -> reply delivered
  -> replyOperation.complete()
  -> memory flush dispatched (fire-and-forget; registered in pending map)

Next turn:
  -> preflight compaction
       -> await previous turn's pending flush (so compaction sees post-flush state)
  -> ...
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: unit + seam-level integration tests.
- Target tests added in this PR:
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > dispatches the post-reply memory
    flush after the user-visible reply path returns`
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > dispatches the post-reply memory
    flush from the reply path's finally block even when the agent turn
    throws`
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > does not block the start of the
    user-visible reply on a pending memory flush`
  - `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded pending-flush barrier > awaits a
    pending flush before invoking compaction on the next turn`
  - `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded pending-flush barrier > rebinds the
    active run sessionId after the barrier when the prior flush rotated
    it, even if no compaction runs this turn`
  - `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded pending-flush barrier > does not
    block heartbeat preflight on a pending flush`
  - `pending memory-flush registry > awaitPendingMemoryFlush proceeds
    after timeout when the flush hangs`
  - `pending memory-flush registry > registers and clears a pending
    flush after it resolves` / `... even after it rejects`
  - `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded > completes the flush turn without
    throwing when an embedded run returns a visible error payload and
    no onVisibleErrorPayloads callback is supplied`
  - `pending memory-flush registry > awaitPendingMemoryFlush makes the
    timeout terminal: on timeout it invokes the abort callback and
    awaits flush settlement before returning`
  - `pending memory-flush registry > awaitPendingMemoryFlush abandons
    the pending entry when the abort callback fails to make a truly
    hung flush settle within the post-abort grace window` — uses a
    flush promise that never resolves and a no-op abort callback; the
    test asserts the barrier returns within `timeoutMs +
    postAbortGraceMs` and the pending entry is removed.
  - `pending memory-flush registry > awaitPendingMemoryFlush stays
    bounded if the abort callback fails to settle the flush quickly`
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > registers the post-reply flush with
    an abort callback that aborts the maintenance ReplyOperation`
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > passes a maintenance ReplyOperation
    (NOT the completed reply's operation) into the post-reply memory
    flush` — captures the runMemoryFlushIfNeeded `replyOperation`
    argument, asserts it is a different instance whose
    `attachBackend(...)` stores the handle (does NOT cancel), and
    asserts the original reply's operation is in a terminal state and
    DOES cancel handles with `superseded`. This is the direct
    regression guard for the deferred-dispatch attachBackend bug.
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > aborts the maintenance ReplyOperation
    when the original reply operation aborts upstream` — exercises the
    one-way abort propagation from the reply's upstream signal into the
    maintenance op.
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > does not register the operation
    in the active-run registry, so it can coexist with the live reply
    operation for the same sessionKey`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > attachBackend stores the handle
    (does NOT cancel it) when the maintenance operation has not
    completed`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > attachBackend cancels the handle
    with 'superseded' if complete() ran first`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > propagates upstream abort into
    the maintenance operation's abortSignal`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > inherits the upstream's
    already-aborted state at construction time`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > complete() does not call
    clearReplyRunState (so it cannot disturb a live reply operation for
    the same sessionKey)`

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: housekeeping run visible error payloads no longer route to the
  user-visible reply on the same turn.
  - Mitigation: text of each dropped payload is forwarded to
    `logVerbose`. Same-turn surfacing can be reintroduced as a deferred
    channel notification in a separate PR.
- Risk: a hung housekeeping run could keep the next preflight waiting
  on the barrier.
  - Mitigation: the barrier inside
    `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` has a wait-cap (30 s default).
    When the cap fires, the barrier invokes a registered abort
    callback that aborts the maintenance `ReplyOperation` via
    `abortForRestart()` (cancelling the attached backend handle and
    aborting the embedded run's `abortSignal`), and then waits for
    the flush promise to settle. The post-abort wait is itself
    bounded by a 5 s default grace window; if the flush still has not
    settled (abort callback threw, embedded runner ignores its
    `AbortSignal`), the pending entry is removed from the registry
    and the barrier returns so subsequent turns are not blocked
    indefinitely. The abandoned flush may still complete later and
    mutate session state; that bounded risk is explicitly preferred
    over an unbounded availability hang. The map entry self-cleans on
    settlement.
- Risk: cross-turn ordering depends on the per-`sessionKey` map being
  correct.
  - Mitigation: the map is keyed by `sessionKey`, which is stable
    across the in-flush compaction (`incrementCompactionCount` mutates
    `sessionId` but not `sessionKey`).

## Summary

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-search.test.ts extensions/perplexity/src/perplexity-web-search-provider.test.ts src/config/config.web-search-provider.test.ts`
- `node scripts/github/real-behavior-proof-check.mjs`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Per-call `model` override now reaches the Perplexity web_search dispatch path and changes the outgoing chat-completions request body.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using a live `node` harness with the real Perplexity provider runtime and undici runtime deps injection.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node` harness executed via `pnpm exec tsx -` that called `createPerplexityWebSearchProvider()`, ran `tool.execute({ query: "OpenClaw docs", model: "perplexity/sonar" })`, and printed the captured request plus result.
Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "captured": {
    "input": "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions",
    "body": {
      "model": "perplexity/sonar",
      "messages": [
        {
          "role": "user",
          "content": "OpenClaw docs"
        }
      ]
    }
  },
  "result": {
    "query": "OpenClaw docs",
    "provider": "perplexity",
    "model": "perplexity/sonar",
    "tookMs": 7,
    "externalContent": {
      "untrusted": true,
      "source": "web_search",
      "provider": "perplexity",
      "wrapped": true

    "content": "
<<<EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="607865b76e61dbfd">>>
Source: Web Search
---
Grounded answer
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="607865b76e61dbfd">>>",
    "citations": []
  }
}
```
Observed result after fix: The provider sent the override model to `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions`, and the tool result reported the same model back.
What was not tested: A direct third-party OpenRouter request; the live path was intercepted locally so the request body could be observed without external credentials.

target.title: feat(cli): add openclaw experimental for toggling experimental config flags
target.body:
> AI-assisted. Tested locally on the pushed branch: focused Vitest coverage for config/CLI/chat/TUI passed, config schema check passed, and the real `pnpm openclaw experimental --help` entrypoint was exercised after the branch update.

## Summary

- **Problem:** OpenClaw has experimental config flags spread through the config schema, but the user-facing toggle surface was drifting toward a CLI-only implementation. That would make `/experimental` unavailable from chat/TUI/channel command surfaces and would duplicate the flag registry outside the config contract.
- **Why it matters:** Experimental flags affect runtime behavior and should use one bounded, auditable config subset across CLI, chat, TUI, and channel plugins instead of hard-coded per-surface lists.
- **What changed:** Added a shared experimental-flag helper that discovers boolean config leaves under `experimental` from the base config schema and schema UI hints. The CLI `openclaw experimental`, chat `/experimental`, TUI command handling, and channel-command registry now use that same subset.
- **Config command policy:** Added `commands.experimental` so `/experimental` can be enabled or disabled alongside other configurable command surfaces.
- **What did not change:** No new experimental flags were introduced. No secrets, network calls, or provider runtime behavior changed.

## Scope Note

This PR started as a small CLI picker, but review feedback exposed a broader product/security boundary: if experimental config can be edited from one surface, the implementation should not create a CLI-only registry or drift from chat, TUI, and channel command handling. The current shape is intentionally larger because it moves `/experimental` behind a shared schema-derived subset and a configurable command gate instead of adding another one-off config editor.

The scope is still kept to the minimum shared surface needed for that boundary: no new experimental flags, no arbitrary config editor, no new network/runtime capability, no SDK export, and no separate per-surface experimental registry.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CLI
- [x] Config
- [x] Messaging / channel command surface
- [x] TUI
- [x] Docs

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: Discord suggestion from Json in #general
- Reviewer concern addressed: Peter Steinberger asked that this avoid config-schema drift, consider `/experimental` in chat/TUI, and keep config editing security-bounded.
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A - new feature and follow-up design correction.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

Focused local tests cover the shared flag registry, CLI apply behavior, chat command parsing/gating, channel command listing, and TUI command handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New top-level command: `openclaw experimental`
- New slash command: `/experimental`
- TUI command handling can list and update experimental flags through the same shared registry
- `commands.experimental` controls whether the experimental chat/channel command is enabled
- Experimental flags are discovered from the config schema instead of duplicated in each surface

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Config schema + UI hints
        |
        v
shared experimental flag subset
        |
        +-- openclaw experimental
        +-- /experimental in chat/channel command handling
        +-- TUI command backend/handlers
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No. The command writes only existing config paths that users can already edit through config tooling.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes, `/experimental` is a new configurable command surface. It is gated by `commands.experimental` and is limited to schema-discovered boolean config leaves whose path contains an `experimental` segment.
- Data access scope changed? No.
- Security boundary: this does not make arbitrary config editable from chat/TUI. The editable subset is derived from the config schema and narrowed to experimental boolean leaves only.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu source checkout
- Runtime: Node 22 / pnpm
- Branch SHA: `43d40ba3d7c0`

### Commands run locally

```text
pnpm config:schema:check
pnpm test src/config/experimental-flags.test.ts src/commands/experimental.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-parse.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-gating.test.ts src/auto-reply/commands-registry.test.ts src/tui/commands.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts
pnpm openclaw experimental --help
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-experimental-proof.json pnpm openclaw experimental
```

### Expected

The schema-derived experimental flag subset is stable, the CLI/chat/TUI surfaces use it consistently, `/experimental` obeys command gating, and the real CLI entrypoint resolves the new `experimental` command.

### Actual

Verified locally on the pushed branch.

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm config:schema:check` passed (`[base-config-schema] ok`)
- [x] Focused Vitest run passed 5 shards / 107 tests
- [x] `pnpm openclaw experimental --help` executed through the real repo CLI entrypoint at SHA `43d40ba3d7c0`
- [x] Real interactive CLI write proof used a throwaway config and persisted unchecked absent flags as explicit `false`, including `tools.experimental.planTool: false`

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: schema-derived flag listing/apply helpers, CLI command behavior, chat parse/gating/list/set behavior, TUI command handling, live CLI registration/help output, and an interactive CLI write against an isolated throwaway config.
- Edge cases checked: disabled command gating, unknown flag selectors, no-op flag updates, absent unchecked picker flags becoming explicit `false`, and boolean-only experimental subset discovery.
- What was not verified: production user config mutation; the write proof used `/tmp/openclaw-experimental-proof.json`.

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior addressed: The pushed branch registers `openclaw experimental` as a real CLI command while the implementation now shares the experimental config subset across CLI, chat, TUI, and channel-command handling.
- Real environment tested: Ubuntu source checkout at `43d40ba3d7c0`, Node 22/pnpm via the repo `pnpm openclaw` entrypoint.
- Exact steps or command run after the patch: Ran `pnpm openclaw experimental --help`. Then ran `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-experimental-proof.json pnpm openclaw experimental` with a throwaway `{}` config, selected `agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`, and left the other experimental flags unchecked.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the pushed branch:

```text
> openclaw@2026.5.14 openclaw /home/ubuntu/projects/openclaw/.worktrees/cli-experimental
> node scripts/run-node.mjs experimental --help

🦞 OpenClaw 2026.5.14 (43d40ba) — All your chats, one OpenClaw.

Usage: openclaw experimental [options]

Toggle experimental config flags interactively

Options:
  -h, --help  Display help for command

Interactive write proof against /tmp/openclaw-experimental-proof.json
Updated config:
  enabled agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean
  disabled agents.defaults.memorySearch.experimental.sessionMemory
  disabled tools.experimental.planTool

Written config excerpt:
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "experimental": { "localModelLean": true },
      "memorySearch": { "experimental": { "sessionMemory": false } }
    }
  },
  "tools": { "experimental": { "planTool": false } }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The command resolved through the real OpenClaw CLI entrypoint and printed the expected `openclaw experimental` usage text instead of falling through to an unknown command. The interactive write updated the throwaway config with `agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean: true`, `agents.defaults.memorySearch.experimental.sessionMemory: false`, and `tools.experimental.planTool: false`.
- What was not tested: Production user config mutation; the write proof used isolated throwaway config file `/tmp/openclaw-experimental-proof.json`.
- Supplemental proof: Focused config/CLI/chat/TUI tests passed on the same committed branch state: 5 shards / 107 tests. `pnpm config:schema:check` also passed.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? Yes, adds optional `commands.experimental` command-gating config.
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Chat/TUI config editing could accidentally grow into arbitrary config mutation.
  - **Mitigation:** The shared helper only exposes schema-discovered boolean leaves under an `experimental` path segment. `/experimental` is also gated by `commands.experimental`.
- **Risk:** Future experimental flags could drift from one surface.
  - **Mitigation:** CLI, chat/channel commands, and TUI all read from the same schema-derived helper instead of maintaining separate flag lists.

## AI/Vibe-Coded Checklist

- [x] AI-assisted (marked above)
- [x] Tested (focused local tests plus real CLI entrypoint proof)
- [x] I understand what the code does

target.title: Guard OpenAI image compression for PNG outputs
target.body:
## Summary
- Guard OpenAI image `output_compression` so it is only sent for JPEG/WebP outputs.
- Omit `output_compression` for PNG/default PNG in both direct image API requests and Codex Responses image tool requests.
- Document that `openai.outputCompression` is ignored for PNG outputs.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** PNG image requests failed when `openai.outputCompression` was forwarded to OpenAI.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw canary deployment `bot-129` in the Kubernetes cluster on 2026-05-24.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
- `kubectl -n openclaw rollout status deploy/bot-129 --timeout=60s`
- `/Users/airlin/.codex/skills/openclaw-ops/scripts/verify-cluster-patches.sh --repo /Users/airlin/Documents/Codex/2026-05-20/hi/openclaw-cluster --namespace openclaw --deploy bot-129`

**Evidence after fix:**
```text
bot=localhost/openclaw-gateway:2026.5.20-image-compression-20260523
browser=openclaw-sandbox-browser:bookworm-slim
token-exporter=localhost/openclaw-gateway:2026.5.20-image-compression-20260523

[openclaw-patch] OpenAI image compression: patched 2 output_compression call site(s) across 1 file(s); 0 patch marker(s) already present
[openclaw-patch] OpenAI image compression: patched 0 output_compression call site(s) across 0 file(s); 2 patch marker(s) already present
✔ OpenAI image compression patch drops output_compression for PNG output
ℹ tests 22
```

**Observed result after fix:** bot-129 rolled out successfully, patch verification passed, and the PNG compression guard was present in the running runtime.

**What was not tested:** I did not re-trigger a live PNG generation from bot-129 during this review; the canary verification covered the deployed runtime and patch markers.

## Tests

target.title: Control-plane hardening from Optimus ops findings
target.body:
## Summary

Completes the OpenClaw control-plane hardening project stack from Optimus ops findings:

- ADP-237: structured A2A handoff IDs and return-route tracing.
- ADP-238: session/status diagnostics and safer control-plane visibility.
- ADP-239: channel/runtime boundary checks.
- ADP-240: plugin contract validation surfaces.
- ADP-241: plugin contract validator CLI path.
- ADP-242: non-destructive doctor/probe guardrails.
- ADP-243: unified `openclaw diagnose --json` control-plane API.

Maintainer/ClawSweeper follow-up in latest head `8b70e02b35af448af3123b679a59256d206d920c`:

- Baseline session metrics now use `sessions.list.totalCount` instead of the first returned page length.
- Baseline active-task metrics now page through `tasks.list.nextCursor` for queued/running tasks instead of capping at one page.
- Baseline channel connectivity now uses `channels.status.channelAccounts` runtime liveness, with the old channel summary map only as fallback.
- `diagnose --timeout` is threaded into baseline gateway probes.
- `channels.status` map responses are handled without array-only `.filter()` assumptions.
- Legacy root SDK diagnostic-event compatibility test emits and observes a real diagnostic event.
- Diagnose proof was refreshed from the current head.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Validate the new control-plane hardening surfaces and the ClawSweeper repairs on current head `8b70e02b35af448af3123b679a59256d206d920c`.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw development checkout on this PR branch. Commands were run from the repository root after rebuilding the branch. No destructive doctor repairs, service restarts, live plugin updates, production channel startup, or credential rotation were run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Threads normalized browser snapshot `timeoutMs` through the browser agent action, client/proxy request, snapshot route plan, Chrome MCP and Playwright snapshot helpers, tests, and changelog.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible: current main accepts top-level browser tool `timeoutMs` but drops it before the snapshot client, route plan, Chrome MCP snapshot call, and Playwright snapshot helpers. I did not execute the original live macOS or Browserbase repro in this read-only review.
